cases.case_name: Cooper v. City of Dothan
cases.reference: On June 16, 2015, a recent arrestee in Dothan, Alabama, filed this class action lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Alabama. Represented by attorneys from Equal Justice Under Law, the plaintiff sue the city of Dothan. The plaintiff claimed Dothan violated his Fourteenth Amendment rights to due process and equal protection as a result of Dothan's policy of requiring secured generically set bonds which the plaintiff was unable to pay. The case settled after Dothan began to accept unsecured bonds.
cases.summary: Anthony Cooper sued the City of Dothan in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Alabama on June 16, 2015, individually and as a putative class action under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. He alleged Dothan violated the Fourteenth Amendment by using fixed secured-money bail amounts, typically $300–$500, to jail indigent misdemeanor and traffic arrestees who could not pay before their first court appearance. On June 18, 2015, the court granted a temporary restraining order requiring Cooper’s immediate release, and on June 26, 2015 ordered Dothan to follow new Municipal Court procedures adopted June 25, 2015. The case ended April 13, 2016 with a consent decree requiring Dothan to follow those procedures for at least three years; damages were dismissed with prejudice, class-certification requests were withdrawn, and the case was dismissed subject to enforcement of the decree.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core parties, forum, date, Fourteenth Amendment bail challenge, and resolution, but adds many specifics not in the reference and omits explicit mention of Equal Justice Under Law and the unsecured-bond settlement point.

cases.case_name: Cook v. Taylor
cases.reference: On November 19, 2018, plaintiffs filed this class action suit in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Alabama, claiming that the Alabama law which authorized courts to suspend driver's license following non-payment of traffic ticket without procedural protection violated the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses of the  Fourteenth Amendment. The defendant moved to dismiss the claim for failure to state the claim, lack of standing, and sovereign immunity.  The court dismissed the case with prejudice on May 1, 2019.
cases.summary: Lakendra Cook, Christopher Gray, and Sharon Motley filed a putative class action on November 19, 2018, in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Alabama against Hal Taylor, in his official capacity as Secretary of the Alabama Law Enforcement Agency. They sought declaratory and injunctive relief under the Fourteenth Amendment, challenging Alabama Rule of Criminal Procedure 26.11(i)(3) and ALEA’s enforcement of driver’s-license suspensions for failure to pay traffic tickets without notice, a hearing, or a finding of willful nonpayment. Christopher Gray was voluntarily dismissed on January 22, 2019. On May 1, 2019, Judge W. Keith Watkins denied plaintiffs’ preliminary-injunction and class-certification motions, granted the State’s motion to dismiss, and dismissed the case without prejudice for lack of standing because the remaining plaintiffs’ licenses were also suspended for unchallenged failures to appear.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and captures the filing, parties, and constitutional challenge, but it contradicts the reference on the dismissal being with prejudice and adds several unsupported procedural details and grounds for dismissal.

cases.case_name: G.G. v. Hickenlooper
cases.reference: On February 22, 2016, individuals with mental illness and/or in need of addiction and substance abuse services who were exiting jails, prisons, nursing homes, mental health institutes, or who were currently homeless, filed this class-action complaint in the United States District Court for the District of Colorado. They brought claims under the Americans with Disabilities Act, the Rehabilitation Act, and Title XIX of the Social Security Act. The plaintiffs sought a permanent injunction requiring to require defendants to ensure housing, treatment and other services were brought to scale to meet the needs of the class. On July 31, 2016, the plaintiffs moved to voluntarily dismissed the case against the plaintiffs. The case is now closed.
cases.summary: On February 22, 2016, plaintiffs G.G., E.A.C.W., A.K., T.M., G.C., F.P., E.V., and a proposed class sued Colorado Governor John Hickenlooper and state health, human services, and housing officials in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado. The complaint sought declaratory and injunctive relief under the ADA/Olmstead, Rehabilitation Act, Medicaid Act, Equal Protection Clause, Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act, and Affordable Care Act, alleging Colorado failed to provide adequate community-based housing, Assertive Community Treatment, transition services, addiction care, and discharge planning for adults with mental illness or substance-use needs leaving institutions or facing homelessness. On June 17, 2016, the court substituted Susan E. Birch for Gretchen Hammer as Executive Director of the Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Financing. Plaintiffs filed a notice of voluntary dismissal on July 31, 2016, and the case was terminated on August 1, 2016, without a merits ruling reflected in the record.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the filing, class, core statutory claims, requested injunctive relief, and voluntary dismissal clearly, though it adds several legal claims and procedural details not reflected in the reference.

cases.case_name: Still v. El Paso County
cases.reference: In 2017, a former pretrial detainee in El Paso County, Colorado sued the county in the U.S. District Court of Colorado. Plaintiff alleged that the policy of detaining citizens granted a personal recognizance bond by a state court judge, solely for their inability to pay a $55 pretrial services fee to the county, violated their Fourteenth Amendment Rights to Equal Protection and Due Process. Shortly after the filing of the complaint, the chief judge of state courts in El Paso County, Colorado ended this practice. In 2018, the parties reached a settlement to compensate the Plaintiff and 183 other inmates who had been held according to the policy and for attorney fees. The cases concluded in August 2018.
cases.summary: Jasmine Still sued El Paso County, Colorado in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on November 7, 2017, later filing a First Amended Complaint on December 28, 2017. She brought 42 U.S.C. § 1983 claims alleging Fourteenth Amendment due process and equal protection violations based on the County’s policy of keeping pretrial detainees in jail after they received personal recognizance bonds if they could not pay a $55 pretrial services fee; she alleged she was held 27 extra days after a January 11, 2017 PR bond order. Still sought compensatory damages, attorneys’ fees, costs, interest, and a jury trial. El Paso County moved to dismiss on January 11, 2018, but the case ended before a ruling: on August 17, 2018, Judge Wiley Y. Daniel dismissed all claims with prejudice under the parties’ stipulation, with each side bearing its own costs, expenses, and attorneys’ fees.

scorecard.rationale: The summary clearly captures the plaintiff, forum, core Fourteenth Amendment claims, and dismissal date, but it omits the end of the challenged practice and the settlement compensating 183 others and appears to contradict the reference on attorneys’ fees/costs.

cases.case_name: Howard v. City and County of Denver
cases.reference: In October 2018, a plaintiff sued Denver over its bond fee requirement. Within a few months, the defendants changed their policy and submitted an Offer of Judgment. The case is now closed.
cases.summary: Mickey Howard sued the City and County of Denver in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on October 10, 2018, bringing § 1983 Fourteenth Amendment due process and equal protection claims. Howard alleged Denver unconstitutionally kept him jailed for four extra nights after a judge set a $10 cash bond because he could not also pay Denver’s $50 bond fee, after Denver had taken a $30 booking fee from his funds; his municipal charges were later dismissed. On December 21, 2018, Howard accepted Denver’s Rule 68 offer of judgment for $30,000, inclusive of recoverable costs and attorneys’ fees. The court directed dismissal papers, and the case was terminated on January 30, 2019, after Howard filed a stipulation of dismissal.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference on the suit, bond-fee issue, offer of judgment, and closure, but it omits the defendants’ policy change and adds many specific details not reflected in the reference.

cases.case_name: Simms v. District of Columbia
cases.reference: In 2012, U.S. District Court of D.C. found that the policy of seizing and retaining private vehicles pending civil forfeiture proceedings without providing a post-deprivation hearing was unconstitutional.
cases.summary: Frederick Simms sued the District of Columbia, MPD Chief Cathy Lanier, and Mayor Vincent Gray in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia on May 1, 2012, under § 1983 and the Fifth Amendment. He challenged D.C.’s civil forfeiture practices after MPD seized his 2007 Saturn Aura on May 29, 2011, despite his December 7, 2011 acquittal on firearm-related charges, alleging lack of a prompt post-seizure hearing and an unconstitutional bond requirement initially over $1,200 and later reduced to $800. On July 6, 2012, Judge Emmet G. Sullivan granted Simms a preliminary injunction, holding that D.C.’s failure to provide a prompt post-seizure hearing likely violated due process and ordering release of the vehicle pending forfeiture proceedings, subject to insurance, no sale/disposal, and $1,000 security. The parties settled and stipulated to dismissal with prejudice on October 19, 2012, with each side bearing its own costs and attorneys’ fees; the case was terminated on October 22, 2012.

cases.case_name: Thompson v. DeKalb County
cases.reference: On January 29, 2015, a nineteen-year-old man from Decatur, Georgia, filed a lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 against DeKalb County and Judicial Correction Services, Inc. He asked the court for damages, claiming that the defendants violated his rights under the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment when they incarcerated him for five days in the county jail because he was unable to pay $838 in fines and fees stemming from a traffic ticket. The parties reached a settlement agreement on March 18, 2015. Under the proposed settlement, the defendants will pay a lump sum of $70,000 for damages and attorneys' fees. The chief judge of the DeKalb County Recorder's Court also agreed to take measures to ensure that the court's judges and personnel respect the rights of people who cannot afford the probation fines and fees. Such measures include adopting "bench cards" to inform judges of probationers' rights and conducting trainings for court personnel. The case is now closed.
cases.summary: On January 29, 2015, Kevin Thompson sued DeKalb County, Georgia, Chief Judge Nelly Withers in her official capacity, and Judicial Correction Services, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia. He brought § 1983 claims alleging Fourteenth Amendment due process and equal protection violations after he was jailed for five days in December 2014 for inability to pay $838 in traffic-related fines and fees, allegedly without counsel or a pre-jailing indigency hearing. The parties settled: defendants denied liability but agreed to pay a total of $70,000 to Thompson and his counsel and to implement policy changes including revised probation-revocation notices, judge “bench card” procedures, and training on counsel and indigency-hearing rights. Thompson filed a stipulation of dismissal with prejudice on April 3, 2015, and the case was closed on April 6, 2015.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the parties, § 1983 Fourteenth Amendment claims, jailing for inability to pay, settlement amount, reforms, and closure clearly, though it adds details not in the reference and slightly differs by naming the chief judge as a sued defendant.

cases.case_name: Edwards v. Red Hills Community Probation
cases.reference: On April 10, 2015, five indigent probationers filed this civil rights class action lawsuit against a private probation company operating in two Georgia municipal courts.  The plaintiffs alleged that employees of Red Hills Community Probation routinely seize and detain indigent probationers in local courthouses until they or their family members make a payment on their fines and fees. In addition, the plaintiffs alleged that Red Hills employees use threats and misrepresentations to coerce people to report and pay after their probation sentences are complete.  This case is ongoing.
cases.summary: On April 10, 2015, Adel Edwards, Fred Barber, Vera Cheeks, Ulysses West, and James H. Davis, Jr. sued Red Hills Community Probation, its officers, the Cities of Pelham and Bainbridge, and related police officials in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Georgia. They alleged under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and Georgia law that indigent municipal-court defendants were detained or jailed to coerce same-day payment of fines and probation fees, and that Red Hills kept demanding payment/reporting after probation terms expired. The complaint cited incidents including Edwards being jailed until a friend paid $250, Barber being held until $280 was paid, and Cheeks being held until her fiancé raised $50. The case settled and was dismissed: the court entered consent/settlement orders in October 2015 and February 1, 2016, including a Pelham order barring the challenged policies and requiring probation-supervision procedures, and the docket shows the case terminated on March 2, 2016.

scorecard.rationale: The summary clearly captures the filing date, Red Hills, indigent probationer allegations, and post-probation coercion claims, but it adds many unsupported details and directly contradicts the reference by saying the case settled and was terminated rather than ongoing.

cases.case_name: Brucker v. The City of Doraville
cases.reference: On May 23, 2018, four individuals who had been ticketed or threatened to be ticketed by the City of Doraville filed this lawsuit in U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia. The case was assigned to Judge Richard W. Story. Represented by Strickland Brockington Lewis, LLP, and the Institute for Justice, the plaintiffs sued the City of Doraville under 42 U.S.C. Section 1983 and the Declaratory Judgments Act. The plaintiffs alleged that the city’s policy and practice of using its law enforcement and municipal court system for revenue generation violated the plaintiffs' due process rights. The plaintiffs sought declarative and injunctive relief from Doraville’s policy and practice of budgeting to receive revenues from fines and fees. The defendant sought to dismiss the case on July 2, 2018; the court denied the motion to dismiss on July 9, 2019. This case is ongoing.
cases.summary: Hilda Brucker, Jeffery Thornton, Janice Craig, and Byron Billingsley sued the City of Doraville in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia on May 23, 2018, under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. They alleged Doraville violated the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause by relying on municipal-court fines, fees, and forfeitures—allegedly $3 million or more annually and 17–30% of city revenue—creating financial incentives for police, prosecutors, and municipal judges to ticket, prosecute, convict, and fine people. Plaintiffs sought declaratory and injunctive relief, nominal damages of $1, and fees. Judge Richard W. Story denied Doraville’s motion to dismiss on April 1, 2019, and again after reconsideration on July 9, 2019; the case then proceeded into discovery, with discovery scheduled to close June 8, 2020 and summary-judgment briefing set for July–September 2020.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main parties, claims, court, judge, relief, and denial of dismissal, but adds several unsupported details and appears inconsistent with the reference on the timing/nature of the motion-to-dismiss ruling and some requested remedies.

cases.case_name: Greiman v. Hodges
cases.reference: This case was filed on November 23, 2013 and alleges that the Iowa Department of Corrections violated an inmate’s right to due process and protection from cruel and unusual punishment by denying him a meaningful opportunity for parole.  The inmate was a juvenile offender, but was sentenced to a mandatory adult life sentence without parole.  The Supreme Court eventually deemed it unconstitutional to give juveniles life sentences without parole, so the plaintiff was resentenced to life with parole and was applying to be released based on demonstrated maturity and rehabilitation.  The case is still ongoing.
cases.summary: Blair J. Greiman sued the Iowa Board of Parole, its chair (Jason Carlstrom, later John F. Hodges), Iowa Department of Corrections Director John R. Baldwin, and the Iowa Department of Corrections in Iowa District Court for Polk County; defendants removed the case to the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Iowa on December 23, 2013. Greiman, convicted of first-degree kidnapping committed at age 16 and resentenced in 2012 from life without parole to life with parole eligibility, brought § 1983 and Iowa constitutional claims alleging cruel and unusual punishment and due process violations because parole was denied based on the seriousness of the offense and he could not access required sex-offender treatment. On January 15, 2015, Judge Robert W. Pratt denied defendants’ motion to dismiss, holding the claims plausibly alleged denial of a meaningful opportunity for release under Graham. The case was later dismissed without prejudice on October 4, 2017, after Greiman filed a stipulation of dismissal.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and captures the juvenile-life-with-parole due process/Eighth Amendment theory, but it contradicts the reference by saying the case was dismissed rather than ongoing and adds several details not supported by the reference.

cases.case_name: Associated Press v. Otter
cases.reference: On May 22, 2012, several national and local news agencies filed a § 1983 lawsuit against the state of Idaho, alleging that the policy of the Idaho Department of Correction to prohibit public viewing of the preparatory phase of the execution process, during which the inmate is restrained and the IV is inserted, violates the First Amendment right to free speech and the Fourteenth Amendment right to due process. The District Court denied plaintiffs' motion for a preliminary injunction on June 5, 2012, but the Ninth Circuit reversed the denial, on June 8, in time for the injunction to apply to the scheduled execution of Richard Leavitt.  On July 10, the District Court entered a permanent injunction ordering the Idaho Department of Corrections to modify their execution procedures to allow witnesses to the execution throughout the entire execution process.
cases.summary: On May 22, 2012, The Associated Press, Idaho Statesman Publishing, Lee Enterprises, The Idaho Press Club, Pioneer Newspapers, TPC Holdings, Boise Weekly’s publisher, Cowles Publishing, and Idahoans for Openness in Government sued Idaho Governor C.L. “Butch” Otter and Idaho correction officials in the U.S. District Court for the District of Idaho. The plaintiffs brought a 42 U.S.C. § 1983 First Amendment challenge to Idaho Department of Correction execution procedures that barred media and public witnesses from viewing the preparatory phase of lethal-injection executions, including the inmate’s entry, restraint, monitoring, catheter insertion, and IV attachment. Judge Edward J. Lodge denied a preliminary injunction on June 5, 2012, but the Ninth Circuit reversed on June 8, 2012, holding witnesses had a First Amendment right to view executions from the inmate’s entry into the chamber through death. On July 10, 2012, after stipulation, the district court entered a permanent injunction requiring Idaho to allow witnesses to observe the entire execution from the moment the condemned inmate enters the execution chamber until the inmate is declared dead, and the case was closed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is accurate and well written and captures the lawsuit, injunction history, and final outcome, though it omits the Fourteenth Amendment due process claim and the connection to Richard Leavitt’s scheduled execution.

cases.case_name: Willard v. Snyder
cases.reference: On March 16, 2001, plaintiffs on parole in Cook County, Illinois filed this action in the Northern District of Illinois suing the Illinois Department of Corrections under state law and 42 U.S.C. §1983 alleging violations of their constitutional right to due process. Plaintiffs were allegedly denied their constitutional right to prompt preliminary parole revocation hearings nearby the site of their arrest and were instead transferred hundreds of miles from Cook County and forced to waits months, all according to Department policy. In a stipulated dismissal, the Illinois Department of Corrections changed their policies with regard to parolees arrested for technical violations to be more reflective of those parolees' constitutional rights.
cases.summary: Michael Willard, William Frierson, Gary Moore, and later Douglas Nash filed a putative class action in the Northern District of Illinois on March 16, 2001, against Illinois Department of Corrections officials Donald Snyder, James Cervone, and parole agent Richard Lapidos. The amended complaint asserted § 1983 due-process claims alleging Cook County parolees arrested for technical violations were transferred to Joliet/Big Muddy without prompt preliminary parole-revocation hearings, notice, counsel, witnesses, or hearings near the alleged violation site. On September 13, 2001, the parties filed a stipulated dismissal with prejudice, acknowledging IDOC directives requiring, absent exceptions, preliminary hearings within 10 business days and at a location reasonably near Cook County, with improved notice and witness procedures. Judge Matthew F. Kennelly dismissed the case on September 17, 2001; the stipulation stated it was not an admission of liability.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and captures the core due-process parole-hearing allegations and policy-change dismissal, but it conflicts with the reference on the named defendant and omits the state-law claims and some delay details.

cases.case_name: Doe v. Marion County
cases.reference: In 2012, the plaintiff, a registered sex offender living in Marion County, Illinois, filed this class action seeking to strike down an Indiana law that barred registered sex offenders from using social media. After losing on the merits, the plaintiff was successful on appeal in 2013, when the Seven Circuit reversed and remanded with instructions to award a permanent injunction against the state. The case is now closed.
cases.summary: John Doe, on behalf of a certified class, sued the City of Indianapolis and the Marion County Prosecutor in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Indiana on January 17, 2012, under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. He challenged Indiana Code § 35-42-4-12, which barred certain registered sex offenders from using social networking websites, instant messaging, or chat programs accessible to minors, as violating the First Amendment. The City of Indianapolis was dismissed by stipulation on February 28, 2012, and the case proceeded against the Prosecutor. Judge Tanya Walton Pratt initially entered judgment for the Prosecutor on June 22, 2012, but the Seventh Circuit reversed on January 23, 2013. On March 28, 2013, the district court entered judgment for the plaintiff class, declared the statute unconstitutional, and permanently enjoined the Prosecutor from enforcing it against class members.

cases.case_name: Martinez v. City of Dodge City
cases.reference: On October 21, 2015, a private plaintiff sued the City of Dodge City, Kansas and Ford County in a civil rights action arising under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. The plaintiff alleged that the Dodge City's wealth-based post-arrest detention scheme violated the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment.  The plaintiff sought injunctive relief, compensatory damages, and a declaration that the City's wealth-based detention practices were unlawful. The parties agreed to settle that the City would be enjoined from holding an arrestee in custody after a non-warrant arrest on the basis of inability to post bond. The court retains jurisdiction to enforce the injunction but the case otherwise appears closed.
cases.summary: On October 21, 2015, Lawrence J. Martinez filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the District of Kansas against the City of Dodge City and Ford County. He brought § 1983 claims under the Fourteenth Amendment, alleging Dodge City’s fixed bail schedule ($250–$2,500) unlawfully jailed indigent municipal arrestees, including him after an October 20, 2015 disorderly-conduct arrest, because they could not pay cash bond. Ford County was dismissed with prejudice on April 14, 2016. On April 26, 2016, the court entered a settlement-based declaratory judgment and injunction requiring Dodge City to release non-warrant municipal arrestees on own-recognizance bonds without monetary bond, subject to limited exceptions, and dismissed the class-certification motion, TRO/preliminary-injunction motion, and remaining damages/fees claims with prejudice.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core parties, § 1983 Fourteenth Amendment wealth-based detention claim, and injunctive settlement outcome, but adds many case-specific details not present in the reference and omits the court’s retained jurisdiction.

cases.case_name: Jones v. Clark County
cases.reference: In 2015, prisoners in the Kentucky Clark County Jail filed this class action in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Kentucky, alleging that the state did not afford adequate procedural right prior to unlawfully charging inmates a fee for their incarceration without an order from the sentencing court. The plaintiffs sought a declaratory judgment, permanent injunction of the practice, and monetary damages. The court granted the defendants' motion to dismiss and dismissed all claims alleged in the complaint with prejudice, and all pending motions were denied moot. In 2016, the plaintiffs appealed but the appeals court affirmed the judgment of the district court.
cases.summary: David Jones filed a putative class action on November 30, 2015, in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Kentucky against Clark County, Kentucky and Clark County Jailer Frank Doyle. Jones alleged under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and state law that the Clark County Detention Center unlawfully billed detainees for incarceration costs without a sentencing-court order; after Jones was jailed from October 26, 2013 to December 15, 2014 and his charges were dismissed on April 2, 2015, he received a bill for more than $4,000 and paid $20. On March 11, 2016, the district court granted defendants’ motion to dismiss, rejecting the federal due-process theory and declining supplemental jurisdiction over the state-law claims. The Sixth Circuit affirmed on December 19, 2016, holding that merely billing Jones did not deprive him of a protected property interest and that he could refuse payment and contest any collection action.

cases.case_name: Cole v. Warren County
cases.reference: In 2011, two former inmates of the Warren County Jail filed this class-action lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Kentucky. The plaintiffs argued that their Fourth and the Fourteenth Amendments’ rights were violated by the Warren County Jail’s policy of taking checks and cash from the inmates during their admission and, without their knowledge or consent, presenting the checks to the Bank, which accepted them for deposit into Jail’s accounts at the Bank. The plaintiffs also allege that the Jail retained proceeds of such checks and cash without plaintiff’s consent, and in excess of the amounts authorized by the state law (KRS 441.265) to cover the charges and fees the Jail is entitled to receive. In 2012, the court dismissed the case, stating that the Jail’s policy did not violate the plaintiffs’ constitutional rights. The case was closed in May 2012.
cases.summary: Stuart Cole and Loren Patterson filed a putative class action on December 12, 2011, in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Kentucky against Warren County, Kentucky, jail officials, South Central Bank of Bowling Green, Inc., Ellen Lee Bale, and Tommy Ross. They alleged under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and Kentucky law that the Warren County Regional Jail wrongfully seized inmates’ checks and cash, deposited checks into a jail account without consent, and deducted jail fees allegedly beyond what KRS § 441.265 allowed. The court described Cole’s seized checks as including $33 in 2003 and $200 and $59.55 in 2009, and Patterson’s as a $150 check in 2011, with booking, housing, bond, and handling fees deducted or assessed. On May 29–30, 2012, Chief Judge Joseph H. McKinley Jr. granted the bank defendants’ motion to dismiss and the county defendants’ motion for summary judgment on the § 1983 claim, dismissed that claim with prejudice, dismissed the state-law claims without prejudice, denied leave to amend, and entered judgment for defendants.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately and clearly captures the parties, core allegations, statutory/constitutional framing, and 2012 dismissal outcome, with additional procedural detail that is consistent with the reference.

cases.case_name: Snow v. Lambert
cases.reference: In 2015 a mother of two filed suit against Ascension Parish in Louisiana for unlawful detainment for her inability to pay the predetermined bail bond for her two misdemeanor crimes. After the court granted a temporary retraining order and preliminary injunction against the defendants, the two parties soon reached a court-approved settlement in which the defendant agreed to change the bail policy.
cases.summary: On August 25, 2015, Rebecca M. Snow filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Louisiana against Ascension Parish Court Judge Marilyn M. Lambert and Ascension Parish Sheriff Jeffrey F. Wiley, both in their official capacities. Snow brought § 1983 claims under the Fourteenth Amendment, alleging that Ascension Parish’s secured money-bail schedule detained indigent misdemeanor arrestees who could not pay amounts such as her $579 scheduled bail. On August 27, 2015, Judge Shelly D. Dick granted Snow a temporary restraining order in part and ordered Sheriff Wiley to release her immediately on recognizance, unsecured bond, or other lawful nonfinancial conditions. The case settled on September 3, 2015: defendants agreed that misdemeanor arrestees would not be jailed on unaffordable secured monetary bonds, with most released on recognizance after booking and specified exceptions receiving prompt individualized review; Snow’s claims were dismissed with prejudice, with the court retaining jurisdiction to enforce the settlement.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is accurate and well written with substantial relevant detail, but it omits the reference’s mention of a preliminary injunction and some basic context such as her being a mother of two charged with two misdemeanors.

cases.case_name: Levy v. Louisiana Department of Public Safety and Corrections
cases.reference: On August 16, 2016, probationers and parolees who were deaf or had hearing impairments and who were under the control and supervision of the Louisiana Department of Public Safety and Corrections ("LDPSC") brought this class action in the District Court for the Middle District of Louisiana. The plaintiffs sued the LDPSC and its Secretary under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, alleging that the defendants failed to provide auxiliary aids and services to probationers and parolees who were deaf or had hearing impairments. Specifically, they alleged that the defendants, by denying them access to auxiliary aids, denied the plaintiffs an equal opportunity to successfully complete their probation and parole and to effectively participate in post-release rehabilitative programs, which could (and for at least one plaintiff did) result in a finding that they had violated their probation or parole. The parties reached a settlement in which the defendants modified their processes related to intake, interpreters, record retention, training, and grievances. The court retains jurisdiction to enforce the settlement.
cases.summary: On August 16, 2016, Lawrence Levy, Cedric Hammond, and Bradley Casto sued the Louisiana Department of Public Safety and Corrections and Secretary James LeBlanc in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Louisiana, individually and on behalf of deaf or hard-of-hearing probationers and parolees. They alleged violations of Title II of the ADA and § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act based on failures to provide qualified ASL interpreters and other auxiliary aids during parole/probation intake, officer meetings, home visits, and required classes. The court denied plaintiffs’ motion for summary judgment on March 4, 2019, and the parties reached a settlement at a March 27, 2019 conference. On May 28, 2019, the court entered the settlement and dismissed the case while retaining jurisdiction to enforce it; the agreement requires communication assessments, qualified interpreters for specified encounters and programs, training and grievance procedures, and payment of $425,000 in plaintiffs’ attorneys’ fees and costs.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core parties, legal claims, and settlement outcome, but adds several unsupported procedural and monetary details and omits some reference points such as record retention and the probation/parole violation consequences.

cases.case_name: Meade v. Bonin
cases.reference: In May of 2020 two plaintiffs who were criminal defendants ordered to use ETOH ankle monitoring services in Judge Bonin’s court, represented by the Institute for Justice, filed this lawsuit against Judge Paul Bonin and ETOH Monitoring service in the Eastern District of Louisiana. The plaintiffs alleged 14th Amendment and due process violations under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. The plaintiffs claimed that Judge Bonin violated criminal defendants rights by not disclosing his political and financial ties to ETOH Monitoring service when ordering defendants to nearly exclusively use ETOH Monitoring and then making their parol or incarceration dependent on all fees and dues being resolved with ETOH Monitoring.
cases.summary: Hakeem Meade and Marshall Sookram filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Louisiana on May 14, 2020, later amended May 27, 2020, against Orleans Parish Criminal District Court Judge Paul A. Bonin and ETOH Monitoring, LLC. They brought 42 U.S.C. § 1983 Fourteenth Amendment due-process claims alleging Judge Bonin steered criminal defendants to ETOH for paid ankle monitoring despite undisclosed personal, professional, financial, and political ties to ETOH’s principals, and sought declaratory and injunctive relief including disgorgement/cancellation of fees. The amended complaint alleged ETOH charged a $100 installation fee plus $8.50–$10 per day; Meade allegedly incurred about $600 and still owed about $322, while Sookram allegedly paid well over $1,000. Plaintiffs voluntarily dismissed Judge Bonin without prejudice on August 23, 2020, and on September 4, 2020, Judge Carl J. Barbier denied ETOH’s Rule 12(b)(6) motion, holding ETOH could be treated as a state actor for ankle-monitoring services; the case remained pending against ETOH.

cases.case_name: Ryan v. Smith
cases.reference: In December 2020, four individuals at East Baton Rouge Parish Prison (EBRPP) filed this class action lawsuit alleging violations of their Fourteenth and Sixth Amendment rights by judicial and prison officials. On December 15, the plaintiffs filed a motion for temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction. The request for a temporary restraining order was later withdrawn in January 2021. A settlement conference is scheduled for March 11 and the deadline for the parties to file a joint status report is March 21. The defendants' responsive pleading to the complaint is due on March 22, 2021. This case is ongoing.
cases.summary: On December 14, 2020, Joshua Ryan, Blaze Franklin, Amisar Cyrus Nourani, and Herbert Scully filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Louisiana against 19th Judicial District Court Judges Tarvald Smith, Bonnie Jackson, and Ronald Johnson, Commissioner Nicole Robinson, bail-bond coordinator Frank Howze, East Baton Rouge Sheriff Sid Gautreaux, and Warden Dennis Grimes. The complaint alleges under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 that defendants’ bail practices violate the Fourteenth Amendment and Sixth Amendment by detaining indigent arrestees who cannot pay secured bonds—alleged here as $10,000, $200,000, $13,000, and $30,750—without ability-to-pay findings, consideration of nonfinancial alternatives, or meaningful counsel. Plaintiffs moved on December 15, 2020 for a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction against Gautreaux and Grimes seeking release of the named plaintiffs and relief for the putative class. The TRO request was withdrawn and the withdrawal granted on January 19, 2021; the preliminary-injunction hearing was continued on January 21, 2021, and a settlement conference was set for March 11, 2021, with the case still pending on the provided docket.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core lawsuit, constitutional claims, TRO/preliminary injunction motion, TRO withdrawal, settlement conference, and ongoing status, but omits the joint status report and responsive pleading deadlines and includes extra details not reflected in the reference.

cases.case_name: Humphrey v. LeBlanc
cases.reference: In 2020, three former Louisiana inmates filed this class action lawsuit in the U.S. District Court in the Middle District of Louisiana. Plaintiffs claimed that their overdetentions and the overdetentions of thousands of other former inmates due to slow processing constituted a violation of their 14th Amendment right to due process and that James LeBlanc was deliberately indifferent to these violations. As of writing, this case is still in discovery, and the plaintiffs' class has not yet been certified.
cases.summary: Brian Humphrey filed a putative class action on April 15, 2020, in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Louisiana against James LeBlanc, Secretary of the Louisiana Department of Public Safety & Corrections; a second amended complaint filed April 29, 2021 added Joel Giroir and Bryant White as named plaintiffs. Plaintiffs allege that DOC routinely overdetains people after their legal release dates, including Humphrey for 27 days after an April 16, 2019 time-served sentence, Giroir until February 22, 2021 after a January 26, 2021 revocation/resencing hearing, and White until April 23, 2021 after a March 1, 2021 guilty plea. They assert a § 1983 Fourteenth Amendment due-process claim and Louisiana-law claims for due process, false imprisonment, negligence, and intentional infliction of emotional distress, seeking class damages, punitive damages, fees, and costs. LeBlanc answered the second amended complaint on May 12, 2021; as of the June 1, 2021 docket, the case remained ongoing in discovery/class-certification proceedings, with no settlement, dismissal, or merits ruling reflected.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference, capturing the parties, overdetention due-process claims, and ongoing discovery status, though it adds many details not in the reference and only implies rather than clearly states lack of class certification and deliberate indifference.

cases.case_name: Doe v. Patrick
cases.reference: On June 30, 2014, women civilly committed under chapter 123, section 35 of the Massachusetts general laws, to the Massachusetts Correctional Institution at Framingham, for drug and alcohol dependence, sued the Massachusetts Department of Corrections. They alleged that by being sent to Framingham, as opposed of an in-patient rehabilitation facility, they would not receive the treatment they needed which violated the Americans with Disabilities act. Class certification was granted on January 13, 2015.
cases.summary: Jane Does 1–8 filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the District of Massachusetts on June 30, 2014, against Massachusetts officials including the Governor, the Department of Correction, and MCI-Framingham officials. They challenged the practice of confining women at MCI-Framingham solely under Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 123, § 35 civil commitments for substance abuse, asserting substantive due process claims under § 1983 and disability-discrimination claims under Title II of the ADA. The court certified a class on January 13, 2015, and plaintiffs sought declaratory and injunctive relief barring such commitments to DOC facilities. After Section 35 was amended effective April 24, 2016 and no women remained civilly committed at MCI-Framingham, the court dismissed the case as moot without prejudice on March 31, 2018, expressly allowing future litigation if such commitments resumed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core filing date, Section 35 commitments, ADA challenge, and class certification, but it adds several details and later procedural history not reflected in the reference and somewhat shifts emphasis away from the lack-of-treatment allegation.

cases.case_name: Houle v. Sampson
cases.reference: Plaintiffs were parolees who, as a condition of their parole, could not interact with their own children, or maintain romantic relationships with people who had children. The ACLU and Michigan Child Advocacy Clinic filed a 42 U.S.C. § 1983 action against the Michigan Parole Board, alleging that the conditions violated substantive due process. The plaintiffs were successful in settling the case and modifying their parole conditions to allow them contact with their children and to maintain romantic relationships with the approval of their field officer.
cases.summary: Kevin Houle and Harvey Reese sued Barbara Sampson and other Michigan Parole Board members in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Michigan on February 10, 2009, under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. They alleged that special parole conditions barring contact with their children/minor relatives, restricting marriage and romantic relationships, and preventing church attendance violated the First Amendment, substantive and procedural due process, and were void for vagueness. The case was consolidated with related claims by minor children and later included Kenneth Levy and family members; on June 8, 2009, the court denied defendants’ motion to dismiss or for summary judgment. On April 30, 2010, the court entered a stipulated order revising Houle’s and Levy’s parole conditions to allow unrestricted, unsupervised contact with their children T.H. and R.L. and dismissing the case with prejudice, without costs or attorneys’ fees.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is well written and captures the general § 1983 parole-condition challenge and settlement, but it adds many unsupported specifics and omits key reference points such as the ACLU/clinic role and the romantic-relationship modification with field-officer approval.

cases.case_name: Pierce v. City of Velda City
cases.reference: In April 2015, a pretrial detainee filed this class action lawsuit in the Eastern District of Missouri. The plaintiff alleged that Velda City's cash bail system violated the equal protection and due process clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment because an arrestee's release was conditioned on his or her ability to pay. The parties settled the case in June 2015.  Under the settlement, the City agreed to end the use of the challenged system. Upon motion of the parties, the District Court entered an injunction effectuating the terms of the settlement agreement and a declaratory judgement affirming that keeping detained an arrestee who is too poor to pay a monetary bond violates the equal protection clause.
cases.summary: Donya Pierce filed a putative class action on April 2, 2015, in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri against the City of Velda City. She alleged under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and the Fourteenth Amendment that Velda City unconstitutionally jailed indigent municipal ordinance arrestees who could not pay fixed cash bonds, including Pierce’s alleged $800 bond later reduced to $650. On June 3, 2015, Judge Henry Edward Autrey approved the parties’ settlement and entered declaratory and injunctive relief stating that no person may be held after arrest because they are too poor to post monetary bond. The order required Velda City to stop using secured money bail for City-prosecuted violations and to offer recognizance or unsecured bond release promptly, and dismissed plaintiffs’ claims with prejudice subject to enforcement jurisdiction.

cases.case_name: Powell v. City of St. Ann
cases.reference: On May 27, 2015, a person jailed by the City of St. Ann, Missouri (the City), for failing to pay the fee required under the City's secured bail system, sued the City in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri, arguing that the City had violated the Fourteenth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution, and asking for declaratory relief, injunctive relief, damages, attorneys' fees, and costs. On September 3, 2015, the Judge assigned to the case, Judge Rodney W. Sippel, granted a stay in the proceedings to allow the parties to test the effects of a provisional settlement agreement, under which plaintiff agreed to drop his non-equitable claims, and under which the City agreed to end its secured bail policy and pay the plaintiff a $10,000 fee for acting as a class representative. On May 4, 2018, a Consent Agreement was signed by Judge Sippel, whereby most of the original relief sought was agreed to by the Defendant. The Court retains jurisdiction for two years, which may be extended for one more year upon agreement.
cases.summary: Kellen Powell filed a putative class action against the City of St. Ann in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri on May 27, 2015. He alleged under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and the Fourteenth Amendment that St. Ann unlawfully jailed indigent municipal/traffic arrestees who could not pay preset cash bonds, including Powell’s alleged $300 bond after his May 21, 2015 arrest. The parties settled Powell’s non-equitable claims, with the City agreeing to pay him $10,000 while denying liability, and those claims were dismissed with prejudice on September 3, 2015. After an extended stay, the court entered a consent judgment on May 4, 2018 requiring St. Ann to stop using secured money bail for covered arrestees except in limited circumstances and to provide recognizance or unsecured-bond release procedures; the docket lists the case as closed that day.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core parties, constitutional bail challenge, settlement, and consent judgment, but adds several unreferenced specifics and misses details such as the court’s retained jurisdiction and the full requested relief.

cases.case_name: Thompson v. Moss Point, Mississippi
cases.reference: On June 12, 2015, a person jailed by the City of Moss Point, Mississippi (the City) for failing to pay a secured bail fee sued the City in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Mississippi, arguing that the City had violated the Fourteenth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution. The plaintiff sought declaratory relief, injunctive relief, damages, attorneys' fees, and costs. On November 12, 2015, District Judge Louis Guirola Jr. entered a final judgement ordering the parties to follow a settlement agreement that they had reached earlier, under which the City agreed to end its secured bail policy. The case is now closed.
cases.summary: Chevon Elizabeth Thompson sued the City of Moss Point, Mississippi, in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Mississippi on June 12, 2015, bringing a putative § 1983 class action challenging the City’s post-arrest secured money bail schedule. Thompson alleged that after her June 11, 2015 misdemeanor arrest, Moss Point kept her jailed because she could not afford the $3,200 bond set under a fixed schedule, violating the Fourteenth Amendment’s due process and equal protection guarantees. On November 6, 2015, the court entered a declaratory judgment stating that indigent arrestees may not be held in custody solely because they are too poor to post monetary bond. On November 12, 2015, the court entered final judgment approving the parties’ settlement, dismissing the claims with prejudice, and enjoining Moss Point from using secured money bail for City-prosecuted offenses and from jailing people for nonpayment absent required constitutional procedures.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the parties, Fourteenth Amendment bail challenge, settlement, and end of the secured bail policy, but adds several specific facts and procedural details not present in the reference.

cases.case_name: Allison v. Allen
cases.reference: On November 12, 2019, three indigent individuals awaiting trial and confined in the Alamance County jail in North Carolina filed a class-action lawsuit to challenge Alamance County’s wealth-based bail-setting and detention system. This lawsuit was filed in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of North Carolina. Represented by the ACLU and the Civil Rights Corps, the plaintiffs argued that they, unlike wealthier individuals awaiting trial, remain detained and have been denied their fundamental constitutional rights and basic procedural protections simply as a result of their inability to pay bail. The parties are in the process of reaching a settlement. On March 2, 2020, they filed a joint status report and motion to stay indicating that they no longer need a preliminary injunction hearing and requesting that filing deadlines be delayed to April 30, 2020 to allow finalization of their interim agreement and consent preliminary injunction. The judge granted the joint motion to stay. As of March 12, 2020, this case is going.
cases.summary: Lea Allison, Antonio Harrell, and Katherine Guill filed a putative class action on November 12, 2019, in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of North Carolina against Alamance County judicial officials, magistrates, Senior Resident Superior Court Judge D. Thomas Lambeth Jr., and Sheriff Terry S. Johnson. They brought § 1983 claims alleging Alamance County’s secured-money-bail practices caused unconstitutional wealth-based pretrial detention in violation of the Fourteenth Amendment and denied counsel in violation of the Sixth Amendment. The court denied plaintiffs’ temporary restraining order on November 14, 2019, but on May 8, 2020, entered a consent preliminary injunction requiring individualized bail hearings, ability-to-pay findings, counsel for first appearances, and limits on the Sheriff’s enforcement of secured bonds resulting in detention. The docket reflects the case as stayed, with the consent injunction remaining in effect until further court order.

scorecard.rationale: The summary clearly captures the filing, court, core wealth-based bail claims, and general procedural posture, but it adds several unverified or later procedural details not in the reference and does not accurately reflect the reference’s March 2020 settlement/stay status.

cases.case_name: Black Love Resists in the Rust v. City of Buffalo
cases.reference: On June 28, 2018, Black Love Resists in the Rust and four individual plaintiffs filed this class action lawsuit against the City of Buffalo in the Western District of New York. They claimed that the Buffalo Police Department disproportionately used vehicle checkpoints in Black and Latino communities to generate revenue, resulting in disparate impact on Black and Latino people. The plaintiffs sought an injunction to end and remediate these traffic enforcement practices, as well as monetary damages for the financial harm, humiliation, and loss of liberty and property. The plaintiffs filed an amended complaint on May 21, 2020; the case is ongoing.
cases.summary: On June 28, 2018, Black Love Resists in the Rust, Dorethea Franklin, Taniqua Simmons, De’Jon Hall, and Jane Doe filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of New York against the City of Buffalo, Mayor Byron B. Brown, Buffalo police officials, and officers. The complaint asserts § 1983 Fourth and Fourteenth Amendment claims and a Title VI claim, alleging Buffalo used suspicionless traffic checkpoints and aggressive ticketing in Black and Latino neighborhoods for general crime control and municipal revenue. Plaintiffs seek injunctive relief, class certification, damages for a proposed damages class, and fees; the complaint cites, among other examples, Jane Doe’s $446 in fines and $450 driver-responsibility assessment. Defendants answered on July 30, 2018, and as of late January 2019 the case remained pending in discovery, with mediation selected and dispositive motions scheduled for October 9, 2019.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core checkpoint/disparate-impact allegations and requested relief, but adds unsupported or potentially inconsistent details about defendants, claims, fines, and 2019 procedural posture while omitting the 2020 amended complaint and current ongoing status.

cases.case_name: Harold v. Richards
cases.reference: In January 2018, two Philadelphia residents with drug convictions filed this class action in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania. The plaintiffs alleged that a Pennsylvania statute automatically suspending the licenses of drug offenders upon conviction violated their Equal Protection and Due Process rights under the Fourteenth Amendment. Nine months later, the judge presiding over the case granted a motion to dismiss, ending all of the plaintiffs' claims with prejudice. The plaintiffs did not appeal and the case was closed in 2018.
cases.summary: On January 10, 2018, Russell Harold and Sean Williams filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania against Governor Tom Wolf and PennDOT officials Leslie Richards, Leo Bagley, and Kurt Myers in their official capacities. The complaint, brought under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, challenged Pennsylvania’s automatic driver’s-license suspensions for drug convictions under 75 Pa. Cons. Stat. § 1532(c), alleging violations of equal protection, procedural due process, and substantive due process/right to travel, and sought declaratory and injunctive relief and license reinstatement. Plaintiffs also moved for a preliminary injunction and class certification. On September 25, 2018, Judge Cynthia M. Rufe granted defendants’ motion to dismiss, dismissed the complaint with prejudice, denied the preliminary-injunction and class-certification motions as moot, and closed the case.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and consistent with the reference while adding plausible procedural detail, but it omits the reference’s point that the plaintiffs did not appeal.

cases.case_name: Brown v. Transportation Security Administration
cases.reference: The plaintiffs filed this putative class action litigation against TSA and DEA, claiming that the agencies acted illegally and violated class members' Fourth Amendment rights by seizing and confiscating currency without reasonably suspecting them of a crime.
cases.summary: On January 15, 2020, Rebecca Brown and August Terrence “Terry” Rolin filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania against TSA, DEA, administrators David P. Pekoske and Uttam Dhillon, DEA Agent “Steve” (last name unknown), and the United States. They allege TSA and DEA unlawfully seized $82,373 in cash from Brown at Pittsburgh International Airport on August 26, 2019, asserting APA and Fourth Amendment claims, a Rule 41(g) claim for return of property, and a Bivens damages claim against Agent Steve. Plaintiffs seek nationwide declaratory and injunctive relief against alleged airport cash-seizure policies, return of the $82,373 plus interest, and compensatory damages. As of May 20, 2020, the case was marked stayed, and defendants’ deadline to answer or move under Rule 12 had been extended to May 29, 2020; no merits ruling or settlement appears in the record.

cases.case_name: Johnson v. Little
cases.reference: This 2006 challenge to Tennesee's execution methods was dismissed at the Circuit Court level. The Plaintiff claimed that both execution methods (electrocution by "Old Sparky" and lethal injection - the choices offered condemned inmates) amount to torture. The District Court granted the state's motion to dismiss the case, and on appeal, on December 3, 2013, the Court of Appeals granted a motion to dismiss the case as moot after the state implemented a new lethal injection protocol.
cases.summary: Death-sentenced Tennessee inmate Donnie/Don Johnson sued Tennessee Commissioner of Correction George Little, Riverbend Warden Ricky Bell, and John Doe executioners in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Tennessee on October 4, 2006, under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. He alleged that Tennessee’s electric chair and lethal-injection protocol would violate the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendments, including cruel-and-unusual-punishment, due-process, and equal-protection claims, and sought to enjoin his scheduled October 25, 2006 execution. On October 19, 2006, Judge Todd J. Campbell granted defendants’ motion to dismiss and denied a preliminary injunction, holding that Johnson waived his challenge to electrocution by choosing it, that his lethal-injection claims were moot, and that his equal-protection theory failed. Johnson appealed, but on December 3, 2013, the Sixth Circuit granted the parties’ joint motion to dismiss the appeals as moot in light of Tennessee’s new lethal-injection protocol.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core parties, execution-method claims, district-court dismissal, and 2013 mootness dismissal on appeal, though it adds unverified procedural details and the awkward “Donnie/Don Johnson” phrasing slightly undercuts precision and polish.

cases.case_name: Torres v. Collins
cases.reference: Individuals in the Hamblen County (TN) jail sued the county court and sheriff in the District Court for the Eastern District of Tennessee in 2020 due to their inability to make bail violated their Fourteenth and Sixth Amendment Rights to equal protection, due process, and access to counsel. They filed the case is a class action on behalf of all current and future detainees in the jail unable to make bail. The jail in question received national media attention for unsanitary and overcrowded conditions. The case is ongoing; no class certification or grant of injunctive relief has yet been made.
cases.summary: Michelle Torres, Robbie Johnson-Loveday, Amanda Cameron, and Bethany Edmond filed a putative class action on February 16, 2020, in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Tennessee against Hamblen County General Sessions Judge W. Douglas Collins, Clerk Teresa West, Judicial Commissioners Katie West Moore, Nancy Phillips, and Kathy Robertson, and Sheriff Esco R. Jarnagin, all in their official capacities. The complaint alleges under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 that Hamblen County’s money-bail practices violate equal protection, substantive and procedural due process, and the right to counsel by detaining indigent arrestees who cannot pay bail amounts including $75,000, $6,000, $30,000, and $1,500. Plaintiffs moved for class certification, a temporary restraining order, and a preliminary injunction. The TRO was denied as moot on March 2, 2020, after plaintiffs confirmed all named plaintiffs had been released; defendants answered on March 20, 2020, and their motion to substitute parties was denied on May 12, 2020. The class-certification and preliminary-injunction issues remained pending on the docket provided.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is factually consistent with the reference and clearly written, capturing the parties, constitutional bail claims, class-action posture, and pending outcome, though it omits the class definition and contextual note about the jail’s widely reported overcrowded and unsanitary conditions.

cases.case_name: McKee v. City of Amarillo
cases.reference: In January 2016, two indigent women that served imprisonment terms at the Randall County Jail filed this complaint in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas. Plaintiffs alleged that the City of Amarillo's municipal code created a modern-day debtors' prison and violated their Fourteenth Amendment Rights. In August 2016, the Court granted the defendant's motion to dismiss and ordered the plaintiffs to pay $3,660.00 in litigation costs.
cases.summary: Rachel McKee, Loneta Jackson, Janet Cato, Larissa Coil, Joshua Moore, and Hilario Nayola sued the City of Amarillo, Texas in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas, Amarillo Division, as a putative class action filed January 14, 2016. They brought 42 U.S.C. § 1983 claims alleging Amarillo’s municipal fine-collection practices jailed indigent people for non-jailable, fine-only offenses without ability-to-pay hearings or alternatives, violating Fourteenth Amendment due process and equal protection. On August 8, 2016, Judge Mary Lou Robinson granted the City’s Rule 12(b)(6) motion, holding the cited ordinance could not be the “moving force” behind the alleged constitutional violations because city and state law required indigency findings and consideration of alternatives. Final judgment was entered the same day that plaintiffs take nothing; the class-certification motion was denied as moot, and costs were later taxed against plaintiffs at $3,660.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core claim, court, dismissal, and costs, but it conflicts with the reference by naming six plaintiffs rather than two indigent women and adds several unverified procedural details.

cases.case_name: Lomas v. Harris County
cases.reference: In 2016, two individuals arrested without warrants filed this putative class action lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Texas against Harris County. Represented by the Civil Rights Corps and the Texas Fair Defense Project, the plaintiffs sought declaratory judgment, an injunction, and attorneys' fees. They claimed that keeping people in custody after a warrantless arrest without providing a determination of probable cause based on sworn statements violated the Fourth and Fourteenth Amendments of the U.S. Constitution. Similarly, also claimed that keeping people in custody after a warrantless misdemeanor arrest without offering bond when eligible for release violated the Texas Criminal Procedure Code. On November 1, 2017, the parties settled. The District Attorney agreed to change the process of presenting probable cause statements for warrantless arrests so that an officer now swears to the facts inputted into the District Attorney Intake System (DIMS). The defendants paid the plaintiffs $75,000 in attorneys' fees, but did not admit liability or that they engaged in the practices alleged by the plaintiffs. The court ordered for the settlement to be monitored for three years.
cases.summary: Lucas Lomas and Carlos Ealgin filed a putative class action against Harris County, Texas, in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division, on December 28, 2016. They brought 42 U.S.C. § 1983 claims under the Fourth and Fourteenth Amendments, plus a Texas false-imprisonment claim, alleging Harris County detained warrantless arrestees without prompt probable-cause determinations supported by oath or affirmation. On November 1, 2017, the parties jointly moved for entry of judgment, and Chief Judge Lee H. Rosenthal entered a final consent judgment closing the case. The settlement required changes to the District Attorney Intake Management System so officers swear to probable-cause facts, provided for three years of independent monitoring, and required Harris County to pay $75,000 in attorneys’ fees without admitting liability.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and captures the main lawsuit, constitutional claim, settlement terms, fees, and monitoring, but it mischaracterizes or omits the Texas statutory bond-related claim and adds some details not present in the reference.

cases.case_name: Rucker v. Spokane County
cases.reference: A class of plaintiffs who had been jailed for failure to pay legal debts sued Spokane County, Washington for violating their Constitutional due process rights. They successfully settled the case and obtained upwards of $300,000 in damages.
cases.summary: Betty Rucker sued Spokane County in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Washington on November 20, 2012, bringing a putative § 1983 class action over jail sentences imposed for nonpayment of legal financial obligations. She alleged Fifth and Fourteenth Amendment due-process violations based on agreed “Orders Enforcing Sentence-LFO” presented by non-lawyer court collection deputies without a judicial ability-to-pay inquiry; she alleged, among other things, that in 2008 she served 14 days before her husband paid $525 for her release. On November 25, 2013, Judge Lonny R. Suko granted Spokane County partial summary judgment, holding that the right to a contemporaneous Bearden ability-to-pay hearing before incarceration for LFO violations can be waived. The parties settled: on September 19, 2014, the court certified a settlement class of people incarcerated in Spokane County Jail from January 26, 2008, through preliminary approval for failing to pay LFOs under agreed OES orders, and on February 25, 2015, the court finally approved the class settlement, awarded $125,000 in fees and costs and a $10,000 incentive award to Rucker, and dismissed the case with prejudice.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference on the class due-process challenge against Spokane County and its settlement, but it omits the key point that plaintiffs obtained over $300,000 in damages and includes many unverified procedural details.

cases.case_name: S. v. City of New York
cases.reference: Parents with perceived or actual intellectual disabilities who had been investigated for child abuse or neglect by Defendant City's Administration for Child Services brought this class action lawsuit claiming violations of Title II of the Americans with Disability Act and section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. The case is ongoing.
cases.summary: On October 6, 2017, Josefina S., Deaja D., Cynthia Q., Shantell S., and Bianca M. sued the City of New York in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York, before Judge Alison J. Nathan, on behalf of parents with actual or perceived intellectual disabilities investigated by ACS. The complaint alleges ACS violated Title II of the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act by failing to assess, accommodate, and provide tailored services to such parents, leading to removals, delayed reunification, neglect findings, and termination proceedings. Plaintiffs sought class certification, declaratory and injunctive relief, costs, and attorneys’ fees. The City answered on January 2, 2018; plaintiffs’ September 28, 2018 class-certification motion was administratively denied on September 23, 2019 with leave to refile. As of the latest docket entry on May 1, 2020, settlement discussions were ongoing and expert discovery had been extended to July 17, 2020.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the class action by parents with actual or perceived intellectual disabilities against New York City ACS under Title II of the ADA and Section 504, notes the case was ongoing, and is clear and professionally written.

cases.case_name: Anderson v. Houstoun
cases.reference: Plaintiffs, either relatives of the children or close friends of the children's parents, are adult care givers caring for children whose parents are unable to provide care. Plaintiffs sought declaratory and injunctive relief against the state for failing to notify and provide the plaintiffs with per diem foster care benefits for the children in their care.  This case was filed in 2000, and a settlement agreement was reached in 2005.
cases.summary: Edward Anderson and other Pennsylvania kinship caregivers and children filed a putative class action on August 16, 2000, in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania against Pennsylvania Department of Public Welfare officials and child-welfare agencies/officials in Philadelphia, Lancaster, Monroe, Montgomery, Beaver, and Washington Counties. The plaintiffs alleged under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, Title IV-E of the Social Security Act, the Fourteenth Amendment, and Pennsylvania law that defendants failed to notify relative caregivers of eligibility for foster-care maintenance payments and failed to pay them the same per diem foster-care rates as non-relative foster parents. The court allowed a Second Amended Complaint on May 7, 2004, and certified a settlement class for settlement purposes on October 29, 2004. After notice and a March 14, 2005 settlement hearing, Judge Bruce W. Kauffman approved the parties’ settlement as fair, reasonable, and adequate on March 31, 2005, and the case was dismissed with prejudice on April 1, 2005.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the notice/payment claims, foster-care benefits context, filing timing, and 2005 settlement outcome, but it adds many specifics not in the reference and slightly narrows/misstates the plaintiff group by emphasizing relatives and children rather than adult caregivers i

cases.case_name: Saldana-Neily v. Santa Cruz Sch. Dist.
cases.reference: A paraplegic woman filed suit agains the Santa Cruz School District for alleged violations of the ADA and the Rehabilitation Act. The case settled, but the settlement agreement is not publicly available.
cases.summary: Ernestina Saldana-Neily, a wheelchair user, sued Santa Cruz School District (answered as Santa Cruz City Schools) in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, case No. 5:02-cv-04795-JF, filed October 4, 2002. She alleged denial of disabled access at Soquel Cooperative Nursery Preschool and related Adult Education Parent Program activities, asserting claims under Title II of the ADA, § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, California Government Code § 4450, and California Civil Code §§ 54 and 54.1. Defendant denied liability and asserted numerous defenses, while admitting it was a public entity subject to the disability-access laws. After settlement conferences, Judge Jeremy Fogel dismissed the case with prejudice on August 7, 2003, subject to reopening within 90 days if the settlement consideration was not delivered.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the basic ADA/Rehabilitation Act suit against the school district and settlement, but adds many unsupported procedural and factual details and omits that the settlement agreement is not publicly available.

cases.case_name: Civil Rights Education and Enforcement Center v. RLJ Lodging Trust
cases.reference: Three individual plaintiffs, both of whom require wheelchairs for mobility, as well as the Civil Rights Education and Enforcement Center brought this action for injunctive and declaratory relief against RLJ Hospitality Trust, Inc. Plaintiffs alleged that RLJ violated the Americans with Disabilities Act and Unruh Civil Rights Act by failing to provide wheelchair-accessible vehicles to its disabled guests.
cases.summary: On January 15, 2015, the Civil Rights Education and Enforcement Center, Ann Cupolo-Freeman, Ruthee Goldkorn, and Kenneth Kilgore filed a proposed class action against RLJ Lodging Trust in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California; a November 2, 2015 amended complaint replaced Kilgore with Julie Reiskin. Plaintiffs alleged RLJ-owned or operated hotels failed to provide wheelchair-accessible shuttle/transportation services equivalent to those offered to nondisabled guests, in violation of Title III of the ADA and California’s Unruh Civil Rights Act. On May 3, 2016, Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers granted final approval of a class settlement requiring injunctive relief and monitoring for ADA transportation compliance, and awarded class counsel $128,467.70 in attorneys’ fees and $6,532.30 in costs. Judgment was entered the same day, and the case was closed.

cases.case_name: Blanks v. AMC Entertainment
cases.reference: The plaintiffs represented blind individuals seeking accommodation from a movie theater chain. The case settled through mediation in favor of the plaintiffs. The defendants had to provide reasonable accommodation, employee training, and attorneys' fees to the plaintiffs.
cases.summary: On February 16, 2016, Scott Blanks, California Council of the Blind, Leah Gardner, LightHouse for the Blind and Visually Impaired, Charles Nabarrete, Robert Schulenburg, and Empish Thomas filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California against AMC Entertainment Inc., AMC Entertainment Holdings, Inc., and American Multi-Cinema, Inc. Plaintiffs alleged Title III ADA violations, claiming AMC failed to provide and maintain functioning audio-description/descriptive-narration equipment for blind and visually impaired movie patrons, and sought injunctive and declaratory relief. After multiple mediation sessions, the mediator reported on February 23, 2017 that the case had settled. The settlement required AMC, without admitting liability, to maintain and provide descriptive-narration equipment, train theater staff, provide accessible information about described showings, monitor complaints, provide 200 movie passes to LightHouse for testing, and pay confidential monetary terms for fees/costs and LightHouse. Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers granted the parties’ stipulated dismissal on April 27, 2017, closing the case.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and consistent with the reference, capturing the blind plaintiffs’ ADA accommodation claims, mediated settlement, required accommodations and training, and payment of fees while adding non-contradictory procedural detail.

cases.case_name: United States of America v. Humboldt County
cases.reference: On September 7, 2016, the Department of Justice (DOJ) filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. The DOJ sued Humboldt County under the American with Disabilities Act (ADA) alleging that Humboldt County discriminated against individuals with disabilities by failing to make County facilities, programs, services, and activities accessible. Immediately, the DOJ filed a proposed consent decree. On September 13, 2016 Judge Nandor J. Vadas approved and entered the consent decree that provided a wide array of steps Humboldt County agreed to take to bring its facilities, programs, services, and activities into compliance with the ADA and make the county more accessible to individuals with disabilities within three and a half years. The county agreed to pay $275,000 in compensatory damages. As of July 18, 2020, there has been no additional activity on this case.
cases.summary: The United States sued Humboldt County on September 7, 2016, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, Eureka Division, before Magistrate Judge Nandor J. Vadas. The complaint alleged systemic violations of Title II of the ADA, including inaccessible County facilities, sidewalks/curb cuts, emergency services, communications, and programs, after a 2008 DOJ settlement agreement allegedly expired with most required remedial actions incomplete. On September 13, 2016, the court granted the parties’ joint motion and entered a consent decree requiring ADA compliance measures, facility and sidewalk remediation, training, reporting, appointment of ADA personnel and experts, and creation of a $275,000 fund to compensate aggrieved persons. The decree was to remain in effect for at least 42 months, and the case was administratively closed on September 27, 2016.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main parties, ADA accessibility claims, consent decree, compliance period, and $275,000 compensation, but it adds several details not in the reference and possibly conflicts with the reference’s statement that there was no additional activity after the decree.

cases.case_name: Richardson v. City of Steamboat Springs
cases.reference: Two individuals who use wheelchairs for mobility sued the City of Steamboat Springs, for having inaccessible public transportation and facilities.  The parties entered into a consent decree requiring the city to make its services and facilities accessible, pay attorneys' fees and costs, and pay $12,250 to the plaintiffs.
cases.summary: Timothy Richardson and Jonathan Steele sued the City of Steamboat Springs, Colorado, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on July 1, 1999, alleging disability discrimination under Title II of the ADA and § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act. They claimed the City’s public transit system and other City facilities were inaccessible to wheelchair users, including buses without working lifts, inaccessible bus stops, and barriers at Howelsen Hill and related facilities. The United States intervened on April 20, 2000, as to the transportation-related ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims. The case settled by consent decree entered December 11, 2000, requiring transit accessibility measures, lift maintenance/training, facility modifications, and payment of $9,500 to Richardson and $2,750 to Steele; the docket reflects the case was terminated on August 10, 2001.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and captures the core parties, accessibility claims, consent decree, and $12,250 payment, but it adds many details not in the reference and omits the decree’s requirement to pay attorneys’ fees and costs.
