cases.case_name: Taylor v. Regional Transp. Dist.
cases.reference: Disabled plaintiffs sued a public transit agency for violation of the Americans With Disabilities Act and the Rehabilitation Act, alleging their bus system presented numerous barriers to access, subjecting the plaintiffs to delay, embarrassment, and ridicule by passengers and drivers. The parties entered a consent decree, in which the defendant agreed to remedy the problems with its bus system and pay $250,000 in damages, fees, and costs.
cases.summary: Timothy Richardson and Jonathan Steele sued the City of Steamboat Springs, Colorado, on July 1, 1999, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado, alleging violations of Title II of the ADA and § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act based on inaccessible city transit and other public facilities. The United States intervened on April 20, 2000, as to the transportation-related ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims. The case settled, and Judge Zita L. Weinshienk entered a consent decree on December 12, 2000, requiring accessibility measures for the City’s fixed-route transit system and certain facilities, with court-retained enforcement for four years. The decree required the City to pay $9,500 to Richardson and $2,750 to Steele in compensatory damages; plaintiffs later reported that attorneys’ fees and costs were resolved at a January 30, 2001 settlement conference, and the case was terminated on August 10, 2001.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clearly written and captures the general ADA/Rehabilitation Act transit-access settlement, but it introduces many unsupported specifics and directly contradicts the reference by stating much smaller damages rather than the $250,000 in damages, fees, and costs.

cases.case_name: Kyle v. City & County of Denver
cases.reference: Disabled prisoners in the Denver County Jail sued the city and county, alleging it had discriminated against them by segregating them from the general population, denying access to activities and services, and failing to accommodate basic needs. The parties reached a settlement whose terms are not available.
cases.summary: Baylin Kyle, Rudy Salazar, and the Colorado Cross-Disability Coalition sued the City and County of Denver, including its Sheriff’s Department, and officials Alvin LaCabe, William Lovingier/Lovinger, and Elias Diggins in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on April 13, 2010. Plaintiffs alleged violations of Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act and § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, claiming wheelchair-using inmates at the Denver County Jail were segregated in a medical ward without medical need and denied access to recreation, religious services, education, law library time, commissary items, and other programs. After several settlement conferences, the parties reached a settlement on March 3, 2011. The court approved a stipulated dismissal with prejudice on May 31, 2011, with each party to pay its own attorneys’ fees and costs.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core allegations and settlement outcome but adds many specific details not present in the reference and omits the broader allegation of failure to accommodate basic needs.

cases.case_name: Lucas v. Colorado Rockies Baseball Calub
cases.reference: Plaintiffs were disabled individuals who sued the Colorado Rockies baseball club after it removed accessible seats in Coors Field and raised prices on others. There was a trial held, after which the court dismissed all claims by stipulation. It is unclear, however, whether the parties settled.
cases.summary: Colorado Cross-Disability Coalition, Jeremy Hudson, James Hudson, Carrie Ann Lucas, Heather Rebekah Lucas, Sherwood Owens, Nicholas Owens, Kyle Stubbs, Roanne Kuenzler, and Evan Stutman sued Colorado Rockies Baseball Club, Ltd. in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado in consolidated ADA Title III cases, including No. 1:03-cv-01097 filed June 16, 2003. Plaintiffs alleged that wheelchair-accessible seating at Coors Field’s lower-level box sections was not properly dispersed or integrated, had inferior lines of sight, and forced wheelchair users to pay $100 for accessible infield-area seating while comparable non-disabled patrons could buy seats for roughly $20–$38. On April 2, 2004, Judge Alan B. Johnson denied the Rockies’ motion for partial summary judgment, finding factual disputes and rejecting the argument that ADA regulations permitted the challenged clustering of accessible seats. After a four-day bench trial in April 2004, the parties filed a stipulated motion to dismiss, and the court dismissed the consolidated cases with prejudice on August 31, 2004, with costs and attorneys’ fees allocated by stipulation.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference, capturing the parties, ADA seating/pricing claims, trial, and stipulated dismissal, but it omits the reference’s specific point that accessible seats had been removed and adds many details not confirmed by the reference.

cases.case_name: Ehman v. Home Builders Ass'n of Metro. Denver
cases.reference: Disabled individuals in Colorado, representing a class of similarly situated plaintiffs, sued a home builders Trade Association because its annual exhibition of model homes presented numerous barriers to disabled access. The association agreed, in a consent decree, to remedy the problems with accessibility and pay $75,000 in attorneys' fees.
cases.summary: Joseph Ehman, Kristin McIntosh, and William Joe Beaver sued the Home Builders Association of Metropolitan Denver in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on August 6, 2001, alleging ADA Title III violations at HBA’s annual Parade of Homes due to inaccessible homes, parking, routes, restrooms, vendors, and concessions for wheelchair users. Ehman’s claims were dismissed with prejudice on May 15, 2002, while McIntosh and Beaver proceeded individually and on behalf of a certified settlement class. The court denied HBA’s motion to dismiss for lack of jurisdiction and certified the class on November 15, 2002. The case closed after Judge Edward W. Nottingham entered an amended consent decree on March 10, 2003, requiring accessibility measures for future Parades and payment of $75,000 in attorneys’ fees and costs, with HBA denying liability.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the parties, accessibility claims, class posture, consent decree outcome, and $75,000 attorneys’ fees payment, while adding clear and consistent procedural detail.

cases.case_name: Williams v. Denver
cases.reference: Disabled plaintiffs alleged discrimination at a city amphitheater's parking accommodations. The parties entered into a consent decree requiring defendants to provide shuttle services, additional accessible parking and training for employees. The City paid $50,000 in attorneys' fees.
cases.summary: Kevin Williams, Carrie Ann Lucas, Kyle Stubbs, and Roanne Kuenzler sued the City and County of Denver in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado, alleging that Denver’s operation of Red Rocks Amphitheatre violated Title II of the ADA and § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act. They claimed Red Rocks failed to provide accessible parking and shuttle services for wheelchair users and failed to train staff on disability access; Denver also brought a third-party claim against Staff Pro, Inc., which was dismissed with prejudice on July 14, 2000. On October 6, 2000, Judge Wiley Y. Daniel approved a consent decree requiring accessible shuttles, accessible and van-accessible parking, staff training, monitoring, possible $500 liquidated damages for violations, and payment of $50,000 in plaintiffs’ attorneys’ fees. After an amended consent decree was entered on June 3, 2004, the remaining parties stipulated to dismissal with prejudice, which the court granted on January 27, 2005, with each side bearing its own fees and costs.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately and clearly captures the disabled plaintiffs' ADA/Rehabilitation Act parking-access claims, the consent decree remedies including shuttles, accessible parking and training, and the $50,000 attorneys' fee payment, with no apparent contradictions to the reference.

cases.case_name: Williams v. University of Denver
cases.reference: A disabled law student sued his school for numerous architectural and other barriers to accessibility that prevented him from full participation as a student. It appears the parties settled, although no settlement documents are publicly available.
cases.summary: Kevin W. Williams sued the University of Denver in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on December 31, 1996. Williams, a tetraplegic wheelchair user and University of Denver College of Law graduate, alleged disability discrimination under § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and Title III of the ADA based on architectural barriers, inaccessible policies and transportation, and alleged disparate treatment at the law school. The University moved to dismiss, for summary judgment, or to strike; on August 25, 1997, Judge Richard P. Matsch dismissed without prejudice and allowed Williams to amend, and Williams filed a Second Amended Complaint in October 1997. After discovery and settlement proceedings, the parties filed a stipulated motion to dismiss, and on July 21, 1998, the court dismissed the case with prejudice, each side bearing its own costs and attorneys’ fees.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the disabled law student’s accessibility claims against the law school and the apparent settlement/dismissal outcome, but it adds many procedural and factual specifics not reflected in the reference, so accuracy is slightly reduced despite clear prose.

cases.case_name: Baca v. Parkview Medical Center, Inc.
cases.reference: In 2015, a deaf individual filed a lawsuit against Parkview Health Systems Inc.  He claimed that he was not provided an interpreter in violation of the Americans with Disabilities Act, which severely influenced his communications with hospital staff regarding his critically injured daughter. The case settled for changes in policy and practice.
cases.summary: George Baca sued Parkview Medical Center, Inc. and Parkview Health Systems, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on February 9, 2015. Baca, who is deaf, alleged violations of Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, Title III of the ADA, and the Colorado Anti-Discrimination Act based on Parkview’s failure to provide effective sign-language interpretation while his critically injured minor daughter was hospitalized on July 6–8, 2013. Parkview Health Systems was dismissed by stipulation, and the remaining parties settled in October 2015 without admission of liability. Parkview agreed to pay $65,000 ($30,000 to Baca and $35,000 to his counsel), apologize privately, maintain and report on VRI/interpreter practices, post notices, caption/interpret video information, and continue staff training; the case was dismissed with prejudice and terminated on November 4, 2015.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core facts and outcome clearly and in detail, though it adds many specifics not in the reference and slightly complicates the party posture compared with the reference's description.

cases.case_name: Kurlander v. Kroenke Sports & Entertainment
cases.reference: On November 10, 2016, a deaf Colorado woman sued the Pepsi Center for violating Title III of the ADA, alleging that their failure to provide captioning and other communication services was discriminatory against deaf or hard-of-hearing patrons. The parties settled—the Pepsi Center was required to install LED screens to carry accurate captioning services starting before the preseason of the Colorado Avalanche, starting September 19, 2018
cases.summary: Kirstin Kurlander, on behalf of herself and similarly situated deaf or hard-of-hearing Pepsi Center patrons, sued Kroenke Sports & Entertainment, LLC, later proceeding against Kroenke Arena Company, LLC, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on November 10, 2016. She alleged violations of Title III of the ADA, and initially CADA, based on failure to provide open captioning and effective communication at Pepsi Center events. On August 31, 2017, Judge Wiley Y. Daniel certified an injunctive-relief class covering patrons denied full enjoyment of non-concert events using the center-hung display because of lack of open captioning. The case settled by consent decree finally approved April 13, 2018, requiring captioning on four LED boards for covered non-concert events beginning by the first 2018–2019 preseason Avalanche game; non-class claims were dismissed with prejudice, and KAC was ordered to pay $156,104.00 in attorneys’ fees and $5,637.52 in costs.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately and clearly captures the plaintiff, ADA captioning/effective-communication claims, settlement outcome, and LED captioning requirement, while adding consistent procedural details beyond the reference.

cases.case_name: DOJ v. Amtrak
cases.reference: On December 2, 2020, the U.S. Department of Justice filed a complaint against the National Railroad Passenger Corporation (“Amtrak”) for allegedly violating the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. §§ 12131-12165, by failing to make its intercity rail stations accessible to individuals with disabilities by July 26, 2020, as required by the Act. The case was never properly docketed or assigned to a judge, however, because the government announced its settlement with Amtrak on the same day. The settlement agreement required Amtrak to design at least 135 accessible stations, complete construction at 90 stations, and be in the process of constructing at least 45 stations within 10 years of the settlement date. The agreement also required Amtrak to train staff on ADA compliance, establish a monitor-approved process for managing ADA complaints, and create a settlement fund to compensate individuals who were harmed by Amtrak’s most inaccessible stations. The settlement was monitored by the Federal Railroad Administration and the Department of Justice.
cases.summary: The United States filed materials dated December 2, 2020 against the National Railroad Passenger Corporation (Amtrak) in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia, alleging Title II ADA violations for failing to make existing intercity rail stations accessible by the July 26, 2010 deadline. The complaint sought declaratory and injunctive relief and compensatory damages for passengers with disabilities affected by inaccessible parking, routes, entrances, platforms, restrooms, elevators, signage, and related station facilities. The parties executed a settlement agreement dated December 1–2, 2020, under which Amtrak denied liability but agreed to ADA remediation planning and construction obligations, training and complaint procedures, DOJ/FRA monitoring, and a compensation fund of up to $2.25 million plus up to $127,500 for administration. The PACER docket for No. 1:20-cv-03503-UNA was closed on December 3, 2020 as “CASE NUMBER NOT USED” with no documents filed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and covers the parties, ADA claims, settlement, monitoring, fund, and docket issue, but it contradicts the reference on the accessibility deadline and omits the key numerical station-construction obligations.

cases.case_name: United States of America v. State of Florida
cases.reference: On July 22, 2013, attorneys from United States Department of Justice Civil Rights Division filed a lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Florida against the State of Florida. The DOJ alleged that nearly two hundred children with disabilities were unnecessarily segregated from their communities and the general population by the State, which had placed them in nursing homes. The DOJ sought an injunction requiring the State to comply with Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), 42 U.S.C. § 12131-12134, which, as interpreted by the Supreme Court in Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999), forbids the unnecessary institutionalization of disabled individuals, and mandates that their rehabilitative and medical needs be provided in a manner that enables disabled individuals to be independent and integrated members of the community. In June 2014, the case was consolidated with another pending case, which is in the Clearinghouse as PB-FL-0007.
cases.summary: On July 22, 2013, the United States sued the State of Florida in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Florida, alleging violations of Title II of the ADA and Olmstead. The complaint alleged Florida unnecessarily segregated nearly 200 children with disabilities in nursing facilities and placed other medically complex children at risk of institutionalization by limiting community-based services, using deficient assessment/transition planning, and maintaining inadequate waiver and in-home care supports. The State answered and moved for judgment on the pleadings; Judge Robin S. Rosenbaum denied that motion on May 30, 2014, and denied Disability Rights Florida’s motion to intervene on April 4, 2014. The case was consolidated on December 6, 2013 with A.R. v. Dudek, No. 12-cv-60460, and this member case was closed for statistical purposes on June 26, 2014; no settlement or final merits judgment is shown in the record provided.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main parties and ADA/Olmstead institutionalization allegations, but it adds several procedural details not in the reference and contradicts the reference’s June 2014 consolidation date.

cases.case_name: Council for Disability Rights v. City Of Chicago
cases.reference: This is the largest Title II ADA settlement ever.  It started with a lawsuit filed in the United States District Court for the Northern District of Illinois on October 3, 2005, by plaintiffs with visual or mobility impairments who live, work and for travel in Chicago. They sought injunctive relief to require the City of Chicago to properly install and maintain sidewalks, curb cuts and ramps necessary for mobility of persons with disabilities.  In September 2007, the parties settled. The settlement agreement provided: (1) The City of Chicago will install only curb ramps and sidewalks that meet or exceed the specifications of the Federal ADA guidelines; (2) For the next 5 years $10 million per year in new money to repair and replace curb ramps and sidewalks in high traffic areas not on the city's schedule for repair or replacement. The city will also continue to spend approximately $18 million each year installing curb ramps and sidewalks as a part of the City's annual resurfacing work; (3) The city shall install curb ramps into intersections of each cross street at its intersection with the alteration or resurfacing, so that the altered or resurfaced intersections are fully accessible to mobility-impaired persons with disabilities.  The Court retained jurisdiction to enforce the settlement terms.
cases.summary: Council for Disability Rights, Bruce Moore, Larry Quillinan, Venzelma Cotton, Kent Smith, Doris Stewart, and Margaret Holzer sued the City of Chicago in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois on October 3, 2005, on behalf of themselves and similarly situated persons with mobility impairments. The Second Amended Complaint alleged violations of Title II of the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act based on Chicago’s alleged failure to properly install, repair, and maintain accessible curb ramps and sidewalks when resurfacing streets or altering sidewalks. The parties reached a settlement in 2007: Chicago agreed to follow ADAAG standards, spend $10 million per year for five years ($50 million total) in new funds on retrofitted curb ramps and sidewalks, continue annual resurfacing-related accessibility work, and pay plaintiffs’ counsel $130,000. The case was dismissed pursuant to stipulation on May 3, 2007, and on November 1, 2007, Judge Wayne R. Andersen noted that the settlement terms would be incorporated in a final order and that the court would retain jurisdiction to enforce the settlement.

cases.case_name: E.F. v. New York City Department of Education
cases.reference: Students sued the New York City Department of Education, the City of New York, and the chancellor of the New York City Department of Education to seek injunctive relief under the Americans with Disabilities Act, the Individuals with Disabilities in Education Act, Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, and New York state law. The student plaintiffs are either student in Staten Island District 75 schools or vulnerable to being sent to those schools, which are separated from New York City community schools. District 75 students receive their education in a restrictive setting where they are largely isolated from interactions with students who do not have disabilities. Plaintiffs seek injunctive relief in the form of a permanent injunction against future violations and an order for a remedial plan to reimagine policies, practices, and norms that will permit each student to learn in the most integrated setting possible. The case remains open.
cases.summary: On January 26, 2021, minors E.F., A.S., and L.P. (through their parents) and Disability Rights New York filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of New York against the New York City Department of Education, the City of New York, and Chancellor Richard A. Carranza. The complaint alleges that nearly 2,000 Staten Island students with disabilities are unnecessarily segregated in District 75 schools or classrooms and denied equal educational opportunities, in violation of Title II of the ADA, Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, the IDEA, and the New York City Human Rights Law. Plaintiffs seek class certification, declaratory and injunctive relief, a remedial plan requiring education in the most integrated setting appropriate, and attorneys’ fees, with no monetary demand stated. The docket provided shows the case was opened, assigned to Judge LaShann DeArcy Hall and Magistrate Judge Robert M. Levy, and summonses were issued on January 28, 2021; no ruling, dismissal, or settlement appears in the record provided.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core parties, disability-rights claims, requested integrated-setting injunctive relief, and open status, but includes several unsupported extra details and appears to substitute the New York City Human Rights Law for the reference’s New York state-law claim.

cases.case_name: Brown v. Free Library of Philadelphia
cases.reference: Four blind or visually impaired library patrons sued the Free Library of Philadelphia in in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania, seeking relief under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, and Title II of the ADA. They alleged that the Library's new program to lend e-reading devices was discriminatory, because the Nook Simple Touch devices the library had procured were inaccessible to visually impaired users, and because accessible alternative devices were available. The parties entered a settlement and the case was dismissed. The library agreed to transition to the exclusive use of accessible devices within four years, and to add ten accessible e-readers within 60 days of the settlement date.
cases.summary: On May 2, 2012, Denice Brown, Karen Comorato, Patricia Grebloski, and Antoinette Whaley sued the Free Library of Philadelphia in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania. The blind library patrons alleged that the Library’s federally funded NOOK e-reader lending program for patrons over 50 used inaccessible NOOK Simple Touch devices, violating Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and Title II of the ADA. The complaint alleged the Library had received $25,000 in federal funding for the program and had purchased 65 inaccessible NOOK devices despite available accessible alternatives. The case settled, and on October 23, 2012, plaintiffs filed a notice of dismissal with prejudice; the settlement required the Library to buy only fully accessible mainstream e-reading devices going forward, acquire 10 accessible devices within 60 days, and stop lending inaccessible e-reading devices within four years.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately and clearly captures the parties, accessibility claims under the Rehabilitation Act and ADA, settlement, dismissal, and key remedial terms, with additional details that do not conflict with the reference.

cases.case_name: Voices for Independence v. Meadville
cases.reference: On November 10, 2004, Voices for Independence, a non-profit disability advocacy group filed a lawsuit in federal court against the City of Meadville, PA. The Plaintiffs alleged that Meadville had used federal funds to repave roads and had failed to install curb cuts and other accommodations mandated by the Americans with Disabilities Act. The parties entered a series of consent decrees, wherein Meadville committed to retrofit the roads and intersections, and to comply with ADA requirements on all future resurfacing projects. After several years of providing status reports to the Court and fulfilling all of its obligations of the various Consent Decrees, the Court granted a joint motion to dismiss all claims against the City of Meadville on August 8, 2017.
cases.summary: Voices for Independence and Paul Pecunas, on behalf of themselves and similarly situated persons, sued the City of Meadville, Pennsylvania, in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania, Erie Division, on November 10, 2004. The case alleged Americans with Disabilities Act violations involving noncompliant curb ramps at Meadville streets, intersections, and sidewalks that had been resurfaced or altered. Judge Sean J. McLaughlin granted class certification in part and denied a preliminary injunction on May 3, 2005, and the parties entered consent decrees requiring ADA-compliant curb-ramp construction and retrofitting, including later obligations filed March 11, 2009, to retrofit designated Meadville curb ramps by December 31, 2013 and complete certain corrective work by January 1, 2010. The case was administratively closed on October 28, 2005, and Judge Barbara Rothstein granted a joint motion dismissing all claims against Meadville on August 7, 2017.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core parties, ADA curb-ramp/resurfacing claims, consent-decree resolution, and dismissal, but it omits some reference details such as federal funding and future-project compliance and gives the dismissal date as August 7 rather than August 8, 2017 while adding unsupported sp

cases.case_name: Hollihan v. Pennsylvania Department of Corrections
cases.reference: In January 2015, the plaintiff, a prisoner, filed this putative class action lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania, alleging that the Pennsylvania Department of Corrections systematically denied medical care to individuals with severe eye conditions, including severe cataracts, in violation of the ADA, § 504, and his Eighth Amendment right to be free from cruel and unusual punishment. The parties settled, and the Department of Corrections changed their cataract policy, no longer denying surgery to individuals who had "One Good Eye."
cases.summary: Richard Hollihan, an inmate at SCI Somerset, sued the Pennsylvania Department of Corrections, Secretary John Wetzel, Bryan Hyde, Drs. Rashida Mahmud, John Robinson, Donald Kera/Kern, and Wexford Health Sources in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania on January 2, 2015. He brought putative class claims under the ADA, Rehabilitation Act, 42 U.S.C. § 1983, the Eighth Amendment, and the Pennsylvania Constitution, alleging DOC’s “One Good Eye” cataract policy denied medically necessary eye surgery to inmates. The court denied class certification without prejudice on September 18, 2015, and on January 20, 2016 dismissed Counts III and IV without prejudice while allowing the remaining claims to proceed. The case settled effective February 8, 2017: DOC agreed to replace the cataract policy, evaluate affected inmates, provide monitoring reports for three years, and pay Hollihan $16,500; the docket reflects the case was terminated, with later dismissal of claims against the remaining Wexford/doctor defendants and the attorney-fee motion deemed moot.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the prisoner putative class action, legal claims, cataract/“One Good Eye” policy challenge, and settlement-driven policy change, while adding mostly consistent procedural detail in clear prose despite a minor awkward name notation.

cases.case_name: Damiani v. City of East Providence
cases.reference: On October 27, 2008, a disabled man filed action in the U.S. District Court for the District of Rhode Island against the City of East Providence, Rhode Island and various city officials under the Americans with Disabilities Act, and state and federal employment law, alleging the defendant city created discriminatory terms and conditions of employment by requiring him, and no other candidate, to undergo a rigorous physical examination as a precondition of his employment. The parties settled for $22,000 and the case was dismissed with prejudice on October 5, 2010.
cases.summary: Michael J. Damiani sued the City of East Providence and city officials in the U.S. District Court for the District of Rhode Island on October 27, 2008. He alleged disability discrimination, disparate treatment, and retaliation under the Americans with Disabilities Act, § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, and the Rhode Island Fair Employment Practices Act, claiming the City conditioned his Assistant Harbormaster appointment on a “vigorous” physical exam because of his cardiovascular condition. The case settled on September 29, 2010, for $22,000: $7,000 to Damiani as damages and $15,000 to his attorney, Carolyn A. Mannis, as fees. The court entered a stipulated dismissal with prejudice, with no interest or costs, on October 5, 2010.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the parties, court, core disability-discrimination claim, settlement amount, and dismissal, but adds several details not in the reference and omits the explicit point that no other candidate was required to take the exam.

cases.case_name: Galeas v. Law School Admission Council
cases.reference: In 2014, a female student filed this lawsuit against the LSAC and the ABA for discrimination based on sex and disability. The student claimed that she was not allowed accommodation for her Inattentive Type of ADHD for the LSAT. The case was dismissed pursuant to a joint stipulation from the plaintiff and defendants.
cases.summary: Jessica Galeas, proceeding pro se, sued the Law School Admission Council and the American Bar Association d/b/a ABA on March 14, 2014, in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. She alleged that defendants discriminated against her based on disability and sex by denying or delaying LSAT accommodations for her ADHD, imposing repeat registration/rescheduling and other fees, and violating the ADA, Rehabilitation Act, Title IX, and Equal Protection principles. The complaint alleged she paid LSAC’s $160 registration fee four times ($640 total), a $40 rescheduling fee, an $83 undisclosed fee, and about $8,300 for additional assessments. Both defendants moved to dismiss, but the parties later filed stipulations of dismissal; LSAC was dismissed without prejudice on September 3, 2014, and the ABA was dismissed without prejudice on September 22, 2014, with each side bearing its own costs and fees.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the parties, sex/disability accommodation claims, and stipulated dismissal, though it adds many specific procedural and damages details not reflected in the reference.

cases.case_name: Christensen v. Utah State University
cases.reference: On May 8, 2006, twelve deaf students filed a class action lawsuit against Utah State University and its Board of Regents, alleging extensive discriminatory practices in violation of the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act. The parties agreed to dismiss the case on April 17 2007, having reached a settlement.
cases.summary: On May 8, 2006, deaf students Gary Christensen, Brandon Dopf, Kelli Fletcher, John Galli, Christina Hamilton, Ellen O’Hara, Brent Patterson, Callista Powell, Jonathan Roberts, James B. Smith, Emmalee Sparrow, and Jon Helgesen sued Utah State University and the Utah State Board of Regents in the U.S. District Court for the District of Utah, Northern Division. They brought putative class claims under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act and § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, alleging USU failed to provide qualified interpreters, note takers, and other auxiliary aids needed for effective communication and equal access to classes and university programs. The parties filed a stipulated joint motion on April 17, 2007 stating they had settled the dispute, with each side bearing its own costs and fees. On April 18, 2007, Judge Dee Benson dismissed the complaint with prejudice and closed the case.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the filing date, parties, statutory claims, class nature, settlement, and dismissal, but adds several specific details not present in the reference, such as individual names, court division, exact accommodations, costs/fees, judge, and dismissal with prejudice.

cases.case_name: Johnson v. Gadsden City Schools
cases.reference: In 2003, plaintiffs filed for declaratory and injunctive relief, claiming that Gadsden City Schools had violated Title IX of the Education Amendment of 1972, 20 U.S.C. § 1681 and 42 U.S.C. § 1983 by denying plaintiffs' daughters' equal access to interscholastic and other school-sponsored athletics.  The parties reached a settlement agreement on May 7, 2004 and the case was closed on November 2, 2004.  The parties reached another settlement agreement awarding attorneys' fees and costs on November 5, 2004.
cases.summary: Russell and Mary Johnson, as parents and next friends of their daughters Tabatha, Tanya, and Leighanna Johnson, sued Gadsden City School District/Gadsden City Schools, Superintendent Bob Russell in his official capacity, and Does 1–50 in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Alabama on August 5, 2003. The complaint alleged Title IX and Fourteenth Amendment/§ 1983 violations based on unequal athletic participation opportunities and benefits for female students, including the refusal to sponsor girls’ soccer and alleged disparities in funding, equipment, facilities, coaching, scheduling, and travel. The court dismissed all claims against Bob Russell with prejudice on October 15, 2003, after granting his motion to dismiss. The remaining claims were settled, with the School District agreeing to pay $43,000 in attorneys’ fees and costs, and the case was dismissed with prejudice on November 2, 2004, in accordance with the settlement agreement.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core Title IX/§1983 athletics access claims and settlement/dismissal outcome, but adds many unsupported specifics and muddles the reference’s separate May 7 merits settlement and November 5 attorneys’ fees settlement timeline.

cases.case_name: Students for Fair Admissions v. U.S. Department of Education
cases.reference: The plaintiff, Students for Fair Admissions (SFFA) sued the U.S. Department of Education to compel compliance with its FOIA request related to its investigation of Princeton University. The defendant notified Princeton of the request and the lawsuit, and Princeton then filed a lawsuit against SFFA to enjoin the disclosure of the materials requested under FOIA. The present case was consolidated into that suit, and the parties jointly stipulated to this case's dismissal.
cases.summary: Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. sued the U.S. Department of Education in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia on October 27, 2016, before Judge Tanya S. Chutkan. The complaint alleged violations of FOIA, 5 U.S.C. § 552, based on the Department’s failure to timely determine and produce records requested on January 11, 2016 concerning its investigation of Princeton University in Case No. 02-08-6002. The case was consolidated on May 9, 2017 with lead case Trustees of Princeton University v. U.S. Department of Education, No. 17-cv-485, and filings were directed to be made only in the lead case. On December 11, 2017, the court entered an order converting the administrative dismissal into a dismissal without prejudice following SFFA’s stipulation of dismissal.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference, capturing the FOIA suit, consolidation, and dismissal, but it omits Princeton’s notification and separate injunction action and slightly understates the joint nature of the stipulated dismissal.

cases.summary: The Trustees of Princeton University sued the U.S. Department of Education and its Office for Civil Rights in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia on March 17, 2017. Princeton brought a “reverse FOIA” action under the APA, FOIA, the Trade Secrets Act, and the Privacy Act to prevent disclosure of applicant and admissions materials submitted during OCR Case No. 02-08-6002, which concerned Princeton’s consideration of race and national origin in undergraduate admissions. Students for Fair Admissions, Inc., which had sought the OCR records in a related FOIA case, intervened as a defendant, and the cases were consolidated on May 9, 2017. On July 25, 2017, Judge Tanya S. Chutkan granted an unopposed motion to remand and stay the case for further agency proceedings, and on December 11, 2017, the court dismissed the action without prejudice after a joint stipulation of dismissal.

cases.case_name: Cook v. Florida High School Athletic Ass'n
cases.reference: Parents of female student athletes filed suit against the Florida High School Athletic Association, alleging that it violated Title IX and the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment when it limited the number of athletic competitions that a school could schedule, but exempted boys' football. The parties reached a settlement agreement in which the Association agreed to review any changes to existing policies for potential disparate treatment before implementing them.
cases.summary: On June 16, 2009, parents Silvana Cook, Shari Robinson, Ernestine Brewer, Scott Pirie, Gretchen Goodlet, and Omar Pasolodos sued the Florida High School Athletic Association in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Florida, Jacksonville Division, on behalf of their minor daughters. They challenged FHSAA’s amended Policy 6, which allegedly cut high school athletic schedules by 20% for varsity and 40% for sub-varsity teams while exempting football and cheerleading, asserting sex discrimination under Title IX, the Equal Protection Clause/§ 1983, the Florida Educational Equity Act, and Florida’s Sunshine Law. The United States appeared as amicus opposing FHSAA’s motion to dismiss and supporting a preliminary injunction, but the preliminary-injunction motion was deemed moot on July 16, 2009 after FHSAA board action. The parties settled: FHSAA agreed to comply with nondiscrimination laws, avoid retaliation, limit gender-disparate Policy 6 changes, provide Title IX training, post the agreement, and pay plaintiffs’ attorneys $41,200 in fees and costs; on October 21, 2009, the court dismissed the case without prejudice and retained jurisdiction until December 31, 2010 to enforce the settlement.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the plaintiffs, defendant, Title IX/equal protection challenge, football exemption, and settlement outcome, though it adds many extra details not in the reference and slightly reframes the settlement obligations.

cases.case_name: J.W. v. La. Bd. of Elementary and Secondary Education
cases.reference: In 2010, an Aftrican-American 1st grade boy, on behalf of the rest of the students at his elementary school, filed a lawsuit against the Recovery School District after being forcibly restrained by being handcuffed and shackled to a chair for violation of minor school rules in accordance with school policy. In early 2011, a settlement was reached which involved changing the district's policy on using restraints and handcuffs. The settlement also provided for formal training of school security personal.
cases.summary: On July 8, 2010, J.W., a six-year-old first grader, through his father Sebastian Weston, filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Louisiana against Paul Vallas, the Louisiana Board of Elementary and Secondary Education, Edwin Compass, Principal Daphyne Burnett, and school police officers Willis and Jane Doe. The complaint alleged under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 that officials at Sarah T. Reed Elementary School violated the First, Fourth, and Fourteenth Amendments by handcuffing, shackling, and chaining students—including J.W. on May 4 and May 6, 2010—for minor school-rule violations, and also asserted Louisiana-law claims for assault and battery, intentional infliction of emotional distress, and false imprisonment. J.W. sought classwide declaratory and injunctive relief, plus compensatory and punitive damages for himself. On February 1, 2011, Judge Lance M. Africk was advised that the case had settled, and on February 2, 2011, the court dismissed the action without prejudice while retaining jurisdiction to enforce the settlement if necessary.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference, but it omits the key settlement terms requiring policy changes and training, and its detailed defendant framing does not directly identify the Recovery School District as in the reference.

cases.case_name: Know Your IX v. Devos
cases.reference: On May 14, 2020 the plaintiffs filed a lawsuit against the Department of Education (ED) in the United States District Court in the District of Maryland. The complaint alleged that regulations issued by ED "sharply limit educational institutions' obligations to respond to reports of sexual harassment and assault" and therefore contravened Title IX of the Education Amendmentsof 1972. The case is ongoing with responses to the complaint due on July 31, 2020.
cases.summary: On May 14, 2020, Know Your IX, Council of Parent Attorneys and Advocates, Inc., Girls for Gender Equity, and Stop Sexual Assault in Schools sued U.S. Secretary of Education Elisabeth D. DeVos, Assistant Secretary Kenneth L. Marcus, and the U.S. Department of Education in the U.S. District Court for the District of Maryland. The complaint challenges the Department’s May 6, 2020 Title IX sexual-harassment regulation under the Administrative Procedure Act, alleging the rule is contrary to Title IX and arbitrary and capricious because it narrows schools’ obligations to respond to sexual harassment and assault. Plaintiffs seek declaratory and injunctive relief, including vacatur of challenged provisions. As of June 14, 2020, the defendants had been served on June 1, 2020, their answers were due July 31, 2020, and no merits ruling or dismissal had been entered.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the filing date, court, defendants, Title IX challenge, alleged narrowing of schools’ obligations, and pending status with July 31 response deadline, while adding relevant detail in clear professional prose.

Devos resulted in the rule being vacated, this case was voluntarily dismissed.
cases.summary: Council of Parent Attorneys and Advocates, Inc. sued Secretary Elisabeth “Betsy” DeVos and the U.S. Department of Education in the U.S. District Court for the District of Maryland on August 10, 2020. COPAA brought an APA challenge to the Department’s July 1, 2020 interim final rule, 34 C.F.R. § 76.665, governing CARES Act “equitable services” funding for non-public schools, alleging the rule unlawfully diverted K–12 public-school funds and conflicted with the CARES Act and ESEA Title I. cases.case_name: Shelton v. Anoka-Hennepin Sch. Dist.
cases.reference: Two high school seniors who were in a lesbian relationship filed a lawsuit against their school district when their school cancelled a festival procession because the two plaintiffs had planned to walk in together as a couple. The school district settled with an agreement that allowed the plaintiffs to walk in the procession as a couple.
cases.summary: On January 28, 2011, Champlin Park High School seniors Desiree Shelton and Sarah Lindstrom sued the Anoka-Hennepin School District, Champlin Park High School, Superintendent Dennis Carlson, and Principal Michael George in the U.S. District Court for the District of Minnesota. They alleged that canceling or altering the Snow Days Royalty Court procession to prevent them from walking together as a couple violated the First Amendment, federal and state equal protection rights, and the Minnesota Human Rights Act’s prohibitions on sex and sexual-orientation discrimination. Plaintiffs sought injunctive and declaratory relief, damages, attorneys’ fees, and a temporary restraining order before the January 31, 2011 Pep Fest. After a settlement conference on January 29, 2011, the docket states that settlement was reached, and the plaintiffs filed a notice of voluntary dismissal; Judge Susan Richard Nelson dismissed the case on January 31, 2011.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference, but it adds many details not reflected in the reference and omits the key settlement term that the plaintiffs were allowed to walk in the procession as a couple.

cases.case_name: Lovins v. Pleasant Hill Pub. Sch. Dist.
cases.reference: A former student filed a lawsuit against his school district for ignoring the harassment and discrimination he faced because of his sex and sexual orientation while attending high school. The parties signed a consent order awarding the plaintiff monetary and injunctive relief.
cases.summary: Jeremy Lovins sued Pleasant Hill Public School District R-III and Hugh Graham in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Missouri on June 4, 1999, alleging civil-rights violations arising from peer harassment while he was a student from 1994 to 1998. Lovins claimed the District was deliberately indifferent to severe sex- and perceived-sexual-orientation-based harassment, in violation of the Equal Protection Clause and Title IX; Graham was dismissed with prejudice on February 11, 2000. The United States intervened in July 2000, asserting Title IX and Fourteenth Amendment claims and seeking districtwide injunctive relief. On July 31, 2000, Judge Fernando J. Gaitan Jr. entered a consent order requiring policy, training, reporting, and monitoring measures, and the District agreed to pay Lovins $72,500; Lovins’s claims were dismissed with prejudice, and the case is closed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the former student's harassment/discrimination claims based on sex and sexual orientation and the consent order providing monetary and injunctive relief, while adding clear and professionally written procedural detail.

cases.case_name: A.M. v. Jackson Public Schools Board of Trustees
cases.reference:
This 42 U.S.C. § 1983 class action lawsuit was filed on June 8, 2011 in the United States District Court for the Southern District of Mississippi on behalf of all students who currently attend and/or will attend the Capital City Alternative School in the future. The Plaintiffs claimed that the Defendant school district's practice of shackling students to railings for up to six hours at a time was an unconstitutional form of punishment for noncriminal violations of school policies. 

On May 25, 2012, the Court approved a Settlement Agreement that banned the use of handcuffs on any student under the age of 13, and on all students in cases of noncriminal violations of school policies. It further required the Defendants to overhaul their disciplinary system, and called for extensive monitoring of the Defendants' progress over the next 24 months. 

This Settlement Agreement addressed declaratory and injunctive relief only, and left class members the opportunity to bring individual damages claims against the Defendants.
cases.summary: On June 8, 2011, A.M., through his mother/next friend Jeanette Murry, filed a § 1983 putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Mississippi against the Jackson Public Schools Board of Trustees and district officials at Capital City Alternative School. The complaint alleged Fourth and Fourteenth Amendment violations based on a policy or practice of handcuffing and shackling students to fixed objects for hours as punishment for minor, non-criminal school-rule violations, and sought declaratory and injunctive relief for current and future CCAS students. On May 25, 2012, Judge Tom S. Lee certified a settlement class, approved a settlement requiring JPS to end fixed restraints, restrict handcuffing, revise restraint policies, train staff, implement school-climate and behavioral reforms, and use an independent monitor; the court retained jurisdiction for enforcement. After monitoring, the defendants’ unopposed motion to dismiss with prejudice was granted on July 27, 2015.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference, capturing the filing, claims, settlement, and reforms, but it omits the preserved individual damages claims and adds some details not contained in the reference.

cases.case_name: Williams v. Bryant
cases.reference: In 2017, four low-income African American mothers filed suit against the numerous officials of the State of Mississippi claiming the state failed to live up to the educational requirements set by the Mississippi Readmission Act of 1870. They sought declaratory relief that the requirements are still intact. The case was dismissed in 2018 due to the state sovereign immunity under the Eleventh Amendment. The plaintiffs appealed to the Fifth Circuit which affirmed in part and reversed in part. The defendants requested rehearing en banc, but the Fifth Circuit denied the request. The defendants then filed a petition for review with the Supreme Court and a request to stay the Fifth Circuit's mandate in January 2021. The case is ongoing.
cases.summary: Indigo Williams, Dorothy Haymer, Precious Hughes, and Sarde Graham sued Mississippi officials including Governor Phil Bryant, Lt. Gov. Tate Reeves, Speaker Philip Gunn, Secretary of State Delbert Hosemann, and education officials in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Mississippi on May 23, 2017. They brought a federal civil-rights/education action alleging that amendments to Mississippi’s education clause violated the 1870 Mississippi Readmission Act by eliminating school rights and privileges, including a “uniform system of free public schools,” for their children. The district court granted defendants’ motion to dismiss on March 28, 2018 on Eleventh Amendment grounds, then amended the judgment on January 4, 2019 to dismiss without prejudice and denied leave to amend. On April 2, 2020, the Fifth Circuit affirmed in part but vacated and remanded in part, allowing the claim for a prospective declaration that current Section 201 violates the Readmission Act to proceed; rehearing en banc was denied December 7, 2020, and the mandate issued in January 2021.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and mostly consistent with the reference, but it omits the defendants’ Supreme Court petition and stay request and may overstate that the mandate issued in January 2021.

cases.case_name: Women's Liberation Front v. U.S. Department of Justice
cases.reference: On August 11, 2016, the Women's Liberation Front ("WoLF") filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of New Mexico. The plaintiff sued the Department of Justice, alleging that the May 13 Guidance, which required schools to provide restrooms and locker rooms on the basis of sex, but allowed transgender students access to such facilities consistent with their gender identity, violated the Administrative Procedure Act, Title IX of the Educational Amendments if 1872, as well as the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments. With the election of Trump, the DOJ ended it's efforts to protect trans students from discrimination, and so the plaintiffs voluntarily dismissed this case.

King Jr. in the U.S. District Court for the District of New Mexico on August 11, 2016. The complaint challenged the agencies’ May 13, 2016 transgender-student Title IX guidance under the Administrative Procedure Act, Title IX, and the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments, seeking declaratory and injunctive relief. Defendants moved to dismiss on October 24, 2016, and the case was stayed on November 2, 2016 pending the Supreme Court’s decision in Gloucester County School Board v. G.G. After the stay was lifted, WoLF filed a notice of voluntary dismissal on March 16, 2017, and the case was terminated.

cases.case_name: Ray M. v. Board of Education
cases.reference: A class action suit was brought by the New York City parents of preschool children with disabilities against Board of Education, alleging that these children were denied their rights to appropriate educational services under state and federal law. The parties eventually settled, and the defendant agreed to implement integrated placement options and training, collect data, and monitor compliance. The plaintiff was awarded $60,000, including attorney's fees and costs.
cases.summary: On March 11, 1994, seven preschool children with disabilities, including Ray M., Jackey S., Miguel F., Jose P., Katie B., Angel C., and Benjamin L., sued the New York City Board of Education, city education and transportation officials, the New York State Education Department, Commissioner Thomas Sobol, and Governor Mario Cuomo in the Eastern District of New York as a putative class action. Plaintiffs alleged violations of the IDEA, 42 U.S.C. § 1983, Title VI, and New York Education Law §§ 4401–4410, claiming systemic delays and failures to provide timely evaluations, bilingual services for limited-English-proficient students, transportation, and preschool special education in the least restrictive environment. The court certified the class and granted preliminary injunctive relief on April 24, 1995. The LEP claims against the state defendants were settled by stipulation on November 7, 1996, and all remaining claims were resolved by a court-approved stipulation entered February 25, 1999; the case was closed February 26, 1999, with later extensions of the settlement through June 30, 2002. Fee stipulations awarded plaintiffs’ counsel $545,000 from city defendants and $180,000 from state defendants in 2000, plus later fee payments of $15,098 and $60,000.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is well written and captures the broad class-action disability-education settlement, but it adds many unsupported details and fee amounts and omits the reference’s key settlement obligations of integrated placements, training, data collection, and monitoring.

cases.case_name: J.L. v. Mohawk Cent. Sch. Dist.
cases.reference: A high school student who was verbally and physically harassed based on his sexual orientation and sex brought suit against his school district, claiming that school officials were deliberately indifferent to his harassment. On March 29, 2010, the court approved a settlement agreement between the parties. The plaintiff was awarded $50,000, the cost of three years of counseling, and $25,000 in attorneys' fees. The agreement also required that the defendant retain an expert to review the defendant's policies and practices regarding discrimination and harassment, to review these procedures themselves, and to record compliance with harassment and discrimination complaints.
cases.summary: J.L., a minor child represented by his father Robert Sullivan, sued Mohawk Central School District, Superintendent Joyce Caputo, Principal Edward Rinaldo, and Equal Opportunity Compliance Officer Cynthia Stocker in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of New York on August 18, 2009. The amended complaint alleged that the district was deliberately indifferent to severe sex- and sexual-orientation-based harassment and violence at Gregory B. Jarvis Junior/Senior High School, asserting Equal Protection/§ 1983, Title IX, New York Human Rights Law, New York Civil Rights Law, and negligent-supervision claims. On August 27, 2009, the court entered a consent order providing preliminary protections for J.L. at school. The case settled by stipulation filed March 29, 2010: the district agreed to pay $50,000 to a trust for J.L., $25,000 in NYCLU attorneys’ fees, reimburse certain counseling costs through June 30, 2013, and implement anti-harassment policy review/training/reporting measures; the action was dismissed with prejudice on April 27, 2010, and the United States withdrew its motion to intervene.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is accurate and well written, capturing the parties, harassment-based claims, settlement payments, counseling reimbursement, and policy-related relief, though it omits the reference’s specific requirement that the district retain an expert to review policies and compliance procedures.

cases.case_name: A.B. v. Rhinebeck Central School District
cases.reference: Former students and an employee of Rhinebeck Central High School filed suit against the School District and the former principal alleging that the District had violated Title IX of the Education Amendment and the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment by failing to rectify allegations that the principal had sexually harassed the female students. The parties reached a Consent Decree in which the District agreed to implement anti-discrimination procedures.
cases.summary: On May 9, 2003, former Rhinebeck High School students Beth Ann Van Tassel, Jeannine Surrico, Laura Dewitt, Michelle Wyant, related guardians, and employee Cathy Conley sued the Rhinebeck Central School District and then-principal Thomas Mawhinney in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York. They alleged Title IX violations based on sexual harassment of female students by Mawhinney, District deliberate indifference, and retaliation against Conley; the United States intervened on August 25, 2004, asserting Title IX claims against the District. The court dismissed Wyant’s claims as time-barred in March 2005. The case resolved by a consent decree entered March 24, 2006, requiring policy changes, training, reporting, and monitoring, and a separate settlement requiring the District to pay $152,500 total, including $32,500 to Van Tassel, $10,000 each to Dewitt and Surrico, $3,500 to Conley, and $96,500 to plaintiffs’ counsel. Plaintiffs’ claims were dismissed with prejudice on April 18, 2006, and the docket is closed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and captures the main parties, Title IX sexual-harassment allegations, and consent-decree outcome, but it omits the Equal Protection claim and adds many detailed facts not reflected in the reference.

cases.case_name: United States v. City of New York (Lafayette High School)
cases.reference: On June 1, 2004, the U.S. Department of Justice filed a lawsuit in the Eastern District of New York against the City of New York. The complaint claimed that there had been peer-on-peer harassment of Asian students and insufficient language programs to overcome language barriers.  The parties entered into a Consent Decree on June 22, 2004 that lasted until August 30, 2008.
cases.summary: The United States sued the City of New York, the Board of Education/NYC Department of Education, Chancellor Joel Klein, and Lafayette High School Principal Alan Siegel in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of New York on June 1, 2004. The case concerned civil-rights obligations at Lafayette High School, including services for English Language Learner students and responses to student-on-student harassment based on race, color, or national origin. Judge Carol B. Amon signed a consent decree on June 22, 2004, entered June 29, 2004, and the City filed a detailed compliance plan on October 15, 2004. The docket lists the case as terminated/closed on June 29, 2004, with a later stipulation and order regarding the consent decree entered August 25, 2005.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the filing date, court, core claims, and consent decree, but adds several details not in the reference and omits the decree's stated duration through August 30, 2008.

cases.case_name: New York Times Company v. U.S. Department of Justice
cases.reference: This 2017 lawsuit was brought by the New York Times Company in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York. The plaintiffs claimed that the Department of Justice and Department of Education had violated FOIA by failing to respond to the plaintiff's request for documents relating to the work of the DOJ’s Civil Rights Division on affirmative action under the Trump administration. The DOJ and DOE both produced the requested documents in late 2017, and the parties agreed to dismiss the case, which is now closed.
cases.summary: The New York Times Company and reporter Charlie Savage sued the U.S. Department of Justice and U.S. Department of Education on September 28, 2017, in the Southern District of New York under the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552. The suit sought records concerning DOJ Civil Rights Division work on affirmative action and race-conscious college admissions policies, alleging DOJ and DOE failed to timely respond to FOIA requests submitted on August 10, 2017. Defendants answered on November 9, 2017, and the court set a motions schedule. On January 17, 2018, Judge Katherine B. Forrest so-ordered a stipulation voluntarily dismissing the action with prejudice, with each side bearing its own fees and costs; the case is closed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference on the parties, FOIA claims, and dismissal, but it adds several unreferenced procedural details and omits the key point that DOJ and DOE produced the requested documents in late 2017 before dismissal.

cases.case_name: R.A.G. v. Buffalo City Sch. Dist. Bd. Of Educ.
cases.reference: In 2012, a parent, individually and on behalf of their student-child with an educational disability in the Buffalo City School District, filed this class action complaint in the the U.S. District Court for the Western District of New York. The plaintiffs alleged that the Buffalo City School District Board of Education and its superintendent denied free appropriate public education to children with educational disabilities, failed to promptly provide children with disabilities related services, and failed to provide parents with meaningful participation in their children's educational decision-making process. In 2015, the parties reached a settlement that required more timely speech and language therapy for members of the class and awarded attorneys' fees. The case closed in March 2015.
cases.summary: R.A-G., by her parent R.B., sued the Buffalo City School District Board of Education and Superintendent Pamela C. Brown in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of New York on October 11, 2012, individually and as a class action. Plaintiffs alleged violations of the IDEA/IDEIA and § 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, claiming the District denied disabled students a FAPE by delaying related services such as speech and language therapy at the start of the school year and by denying individualized determinations and meaningful parental participation. On June 30, 2013, Judge William M. Skretny certified a Rule 23(b)(2) class, and the Second Circuit affirmed the class-certification ruling. The case settled by stipulation and order on March 24, 2015: the District agreed to void any policy delaying speech/language services to a district-designated date, allow monitoring in 2015–2016 and 2016–2017 at up to $10,000 per year, provide R.A-G. 10 hours of compensatory speech/language therapy, and pay $165,000 in attorneys’ fees; the action was dismissed with prejudice.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is consistent with the reference, captures the parties, claims, settlement terms, attorneys’ fees, and closure, and is clearly written in professional prose.

cases.case_name: Gwinn v. Tulsa School District 9
cases.reference: Parents, on behalf of and as next of friends of their minor daughters, filed suit against Union Public Schools, alleging that the school district had violated Title IX of the Education Amendment with respect to accommodations and facilities provided to female softball players. Class certification was granted on behalf of all present and future softball players at Union Public Schools.  In a settlement agreement dated March 26, 2002, the district agreed to remedy the discriminatory practices by offering funding and facilities on an equitable and gender-neutral basis for both softball and baseball players.
cases.summary: On June 27, 2001, Ron and Bobbie Gwinn, Marla Stanford, and Jeannie and Barry Whitten, as parents of minor female softball players and on behalf of similarly situated students, sued Independent School District No. 9 of Tulsa County, Oklahoma, a/k/a Union Public Schools, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Oklahoma. They alleged Title IX sex discrimination in Union’s athletic programs, claiming female softball players received unequal funding, equipment, practice scheduling, travel/meals, coaching, locker rooms and facilities, and training services compared with male athletes. The case settled after a December 13, 2001 settlement conference, and on February 12, 2002 the parties stipulated to dismissal with prejudice while reserving the court’s jurisdiction to enforce the settlement. On March 26, 2002, Judge Sven Erik Holmes certified a Rule 23(b)(2) class of present and future female softball players at Union Public Schools and entered the settlement agreement, which required gender-neutral measures including accounting reports, equal coaching ratios and salaries, facility access and improvements, publicity/signage, grievance procedures, and Title IX compliance oversight.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the parties, Title IX softball-facilities discrimination claim, class certification, and settlement outcome accurately, though it adds numerous specific procedural and remedial details not reflected in the reference.

cases.case_name: Pollard v. Rogers Sch. Dist. 1
cases.reference: Parents, on behalf of their minor daughters and all female students, brought suit against a school district, alleging that it had violated Title IX of the Education Amendment and the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment by discriminating against female students in the opportunities, athletic facilities, schedules, coaching compensation and promotion made available to female athletic teams. In a settlement agreement entered into on March 30, 2001, the school district agreed to adopt specific practices so as to remedy the discrimination.
cases.summary: On December 15, 2000, parents of female Claremore Public Schools students sued Independent School District No. 1 of Rogers County, Oklahoma (Claremore Public Schools) and Superintendent J. Michael McClaren in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Oklahoma. The putative class action alleged Title IX and Fourteenth Amendment/§ 1983 violations based on unequal athletic participation opportunities and unequal treatment, facilities, funding, coaching, scheduling, travel, equipment, and publicity for girls’ sports. After a January 19, 2001 settlement conference, the parties settled; on March 30, 2001, the court certified a class of present and future female students participating in or seeking school athletics and entered the settlement agreement as an order. The agreement required Title IX compliance measures including adding volleyball by Fall 2002 if enough students participated, potentially adding ninth-grade fast-pitch softball for 2001–2002, improving accounting and facilities, pursuing a softball-field bond issue, committing $20,000 toward a competition-ready softball field, and paying plaintiffs’ counsel $10,000; the case was dismissed with prejudice with the court retaining enforcement jurisdiction.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core parties, Title IX/Equal Protection athletic discrimination claims, and March 30, 2001 settlement outcome, though it adds many specific details not reflected in the reference and slightly reframes some alleged disparities.

cases.case_name: DH v. Ponca City Independent School District No. 71
cases.reference: In 2006, plaintiffs filed for declaratory and injunctive relief, claiming that Ponca City Public Schools had violated Title IX of the Education Amendment of 1972, 20 U.S.C. § 1681 and 42 U.S.C. § 1983 by denying plaintiffs' daughters' equal access to interscholastic and other school-sponsored athletics.  The parties reached a settlement agreement on April 9, 2007, and plaintiffs were awarded attorneys' fees and costs on September 26, 2007.  While the settlement agreement is not available, media reports state that the defendant agreed to improve the softball field and upgrade the plaintiffs' uniforms, equipment, and locker-room facilities.
cases.summary: D.H. and T.H., and B.S. and S.S., as parents of minor daughters K.H. and K.S., sued Ponca City Independent School District No. 71/Ponca City Public Schools, Superintendent Dr. David Pennington, and Does 1–50 in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Oklahoma on September 27, 2006. The complaint alleged Title IX and Fourteenth Amendment/42 U.S.C. § 1983 violations based on unequal treatment of female athletes, including disparities in funding, equipment, scheduling, coaching, facilities, and medical/training services for girls’ softball compared with boys’ athletics. The parties reached a settlement agreement dated April 9, 2007, resolving the merits while leaving attorney fees and costs for court determination. On September 26, 2007, the court awarded plaintiffs $38,375 in attorney fees and $1,590.33 in costs, for a total judgment of $39,965.33 plus interest; the case was dismissed by stipulation on September 28, 2007, and the judgment was later released and satisfied.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference on the parties, Title IX/§1983 athletics claims, settlement, and fee award, but it adds many unsupported specifics and omits the reported settlement terms about field, uniforms, equipment, and locker-room improvements.

cases.case_name: Randolph v. Owasso School District I011
cases.reference: On February 15, 1996, students filed a class action lawsuit against Owasso Independent School District ("OPS"), claiming that it had violated  Title IX of the Education Amendment of 1972, the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution and 42 U.S.C. § 1983 by intentionally denying  female students an equal opportunity to participate in interscholastic and other school-sponsored athletics. On October 2, 1996, the Court entered a consent decree, which settled each of the claims stated in the Complaint and resolved all claims asserted against individuals who were named in their official and individual capacities, dismissing claims for damages.
cases.summary: Ron Randolph and other parents of female students filed a certified class action on February 15, 1996, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Oklahoma against Owasso Independent School District No. I-011/Owasso Public Schools and officials Dale Johnson, Rick Dossett, and John Scott. The complaint alleged gender discrimination in interscholastic athletics under Title IX and the Equal Protection Clause/42 U.S.C. § 1983, including unequal participation opportunities, facilities, scheduling, coaching, equipment, publicity, and support for girls’ sports. On October 2, 1996, Judge Terry C. Kern entered a Consent Decree requiring Title IX compliance measures such as student-interest surveys, expanded girls’ sports opportunities, comparable facilities, and construction of an on-campus softball field; individual defendants were dismissed with prejudice on November 22, 1996. The court later awarded plaintiffs $32,941 in attorney fees and litigation expenses, plus $120 in costs, and Owasso filed an August 16, 1999 compliance report stating it had added freshman softball and 8th/9th grade volleyball, built the softball field, and otherwise complied with the decree.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the basic filing date, defendant, legal theories, athletics-discrimination claim, and consent-decree outcome, but it contradicts the reference by identifying parents rather than students as filers and adds many unsupported details while omitting the dismissal of damages claims.

cases.case_name: Singleton v. McIntosh County School District No. 1
cases.reference: This 2007 federal lawsuit against the Eufala Public Schools, in Oklahoma, sought declaratory and injunctive relief from the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Oklahoma, alleging that the school violated Title IX of the Education Amendment of 1972 and the Equal Protection Clause by discriminating against the girls' athletic teams. The plaintiff played softball and golf; she complained that her teams had facilities (fields, locker rooms, practice times, playing times) that were much inferior to those for the boys' teams.  On March 26, 2008, the Court Steven P. Shreder dismissed after a settlement whose terms were not disclosed.
cases.summary: Kevin and Katherine Singleton, as parents and next friends of their minor daughter C.S., sued McIntosh County School District No. 1 (Eufaula Public Schools), Superintendent Bill Wilson in his official capacity, and Does 1–50 in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Oklahoma on February 9, 2007. They alleged violations of Title IX and the Equal Protection Clause/42 U.S.C. § 1983, claiming Eufaula discriminated against female athletes by providing inferior funding, scheduling, locker rooms and facilities, training access, and publicity, particularly for softball compared with boys’ sports. The defendants denied discrimination and asserted that athletic facilities and resources were administered in a nondiscriminatory manner. After a March 25, 2008 settlement conference concluded with settlement, the court entered judgment dismissing the action by reason of settlement on March 26, 2008, followed by a stipulation of dismissal filed April 21, 2008.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the court, defendants, Title IX/Equal Protection athletic-discrimination claims, and settlement dismissal, but adds some details not in the reference and omits the plaintiff’s golf-team allegations and requested declaratory/injunctive relief.

cases.case_name: Dyer v. Osage Sch. Dist. 38
cases.reference: Parents, as next of friends of female students of Independent School District 38 of Osage County, filed suit against the school district on behalf of their daughters, and all similarly situated female students, alleging that the district violated Title IX and the Fourteenth Amendment through the disparate treatment of female athletic teams. in a settlement agreement the school district agreed to take measures to remedy the violations and provide equal facilities and opportunities for female athletic teams.
cases.summary: On September 29, 1999, Tom and Brenda Dyer, Roger and Vicki Wheeler, and Greg and Shelly Billen sued Independent School District No. 38 of Osage County a/k/a Hominy Public Schools, Superintendent Gerald Christy, and Does 1–50 in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Oklahoma. The class action alleged Title IX violations and Fourteenth Amendment/§ 1983 equal-protection violations based on unequal athletic participation opportunities, treatment, facilities, coaching, equipment, scheduling, travel, and publicity for female students. The court certified a class on December 2, 1999 of present and future female Hominy students participating in or deterred from school athletics. The parties settled in an agreement entered November 30, 2000 requiring Title IX compliance measures, added girls’ sports opportunities and facility/equipment changes; the court awarded plaintiffs’ counsel $19,500 in fees and expenses, and the case was dismissed with prejudice on January 29, 2001.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately and clearly captures the parents’ class claims against the school district for Title IX and Fourteenth Amendment athletic inequities and the settlement remedying unequal facilities and opportunities, while adding non-conflicting procedural detail.

cases.case_name: McCartney v. Mayes Sch. Dist. 32
cases.reference: Parents, on behalf of female students of Chouteau Public Schools, brought suit against the school district alleging that the school had violated female students' rights under the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment and Title IX of the Education Amendment through disparate treatment with respect to interscholastic athletics and programs. In a settlement agreement, the school district agreed to take steps to remedy the discriminatory actions by, among many things, providing coaching, facilities and equipment to both male and female athletic teams on a gender-neutral basis.
cases.summary: On August 10, 1999, Robert and Cheryl McCartney, Deborah and Michael Johnson, and Daniel and Kelly Jantz sued Independent School District No. 32 of Mayes County (Chouteau Public Schools), Superintendent John Phillips, and Does 1–50 in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Oklahoma. The parents, suing for their daughters and a proposed class of female students, alleged Title IX and Equal Protection/§ 1983 violations based on unequal opportunities, treatment, facilities, coaching, equipment, scheduling, and publicity for girls’ athletics. The court certified a Rule 23(b)(2) class on October 28, 1999, covering present and future female students at Chouteau Public Schools who participated in, sought to participate in, or were deterred from school athletics. After a March 13, 2000 settlement conference, the parties entered a court-approved Settlement Agreement on June 27, 2000 requiring gender-equity measures in athletics, and the case was dismissed with prejudice by joint stipulation on July 17, 2000.
