cases.case_name: United States v. Sch. Dist. Phila.
cases.reference: The United States brought suit under Title IV of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Fourteenth Amendment's Equal Protection Clause against a school district on behalf of Asian-American students discriminated based on their race, color, and/or national origin. The discrimination, enabled by the defendant's policies and indifference, took place over the course of several years in the form of physical and verbal harassment based on the students' race, color, and/or national origin. On December 22, 2010, the court approved a settlement agreement between the parties that required the school district, monitored by the United States, to develop an action plan that involved introduction of training guidelines, reporting requirements, and revision of district policies in order to combat and prevent harassment of students. The agreement also provided for alternative dispute resolution procedures to be utilized by the parties.
cases.summary: On December 15, 2010, the United States sued the School District of Philadelphia and the School Reform Commission in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania. The complaint alleged violations of Title IV of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Fourteenth Amendment, claiming defendants were deliberately indifferent to severe and pervasive race, color, and national-origin harassment of Asian students at South Philadelphia High School, including repeated attacks on December 3, 2009. The parties immediately filed a settlement agreement, which Judge Stewart Dalzell approved on December 22, 2010. The settlement required anti-harassment policies and procedures, staff and student training, reporting and translation measures, a consultant, and crisis protocols; the court retained jurisdiction through at least June 30, 2013, and the docket reflects the case was closed/terminated on December 22, 2010.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and largely consistent with the reference, capturing the discrimination claims and settlement outcome, but it adds several unsupported specifics and omits some referenced elements such as U.S. monitoring, the action-plan framing, and alternative dispute resolution procedures.

cases.case_name: Pedersen v. South Dakota High Sch.
cases.reference: Parents, as guardians ad litem of their minor daughters, and the United States as a plaintiff-intervenor, filed suit against the South Dakota High School Activities Association, alleging that the Association had violated the students' rights under Title IX of the Education Amendments and the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment by requiring girls volleyball to be played in the winter, rather than the traditional fall season, while all male sports were held in their traditional seasons. In a settlement agreement, the Association agreed to implement a plan to permanently switch girls volleyball to the fall season and girls basketball to the winter season, thereby affording female athletes the same opportunities as provided to male athletes.
cases.summary: On June 9, 2000, Steven and Cheryl Pedersen, as guardians ad litem for Mara Pedersen, and Suzie Tolzin, as guardian ad litem for Micah and Elizabeth Tolzin, sued the South Dakota High School Activities Association in the U.S. District Court for the District of South Dakota, Southern Division. They alleged that requiring girls’ high school volleyball to be played in the winter, unlike boys’ sports played in traditional seasons, violated Title IX and the federal Equal Protection Clause; an amended complaint also added a South Dakota equal-protection claim, and the United States intervened on November 7, 2000. On December 5, 2000, the court entered a consent order requiring SDHSAA to schedule girls’ volleyball in the fall and girls’ basketball in the winter beginning with the 2002–2003 school year, reimburse reasonable attorneys’ fees and expenses, and submit implementation reports. The transition plan was approved on August 30, 2001, and the consent order was dissolved by stipulation on June 29, 2005.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately and clearly captures the parties, Title IX and Equal Protection claims, U.S. intervention, and settlement outcome requiring girls’ volleyball and basketball to be moved to traditional seasons, while adding non-conflicting procedural details.

cases.case_name: Madison v. Sullivan Cnty. Bd. of Educ.
cases.reference: In 2000, former high school students filed a lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Tennessee against the Sullivan County Board of Education (SCBE) for deliberately being indifferent to racial harassments committed by other students under its disciplinary authority. On November 28, 2000, the U.S. Dept. of Justice filed a complaint-in-intervention to ensure that the SCBE complied with federal rules for non-discrimination. On October 16, 2002, the District Court (Judge Hull) issued a consent order which settled the lawsuit and dismissed the action.
cases.summary: Michael Madison sued the Sullivan County Board of Education, principal Keith Glover, teacher Harmon Peters, and several students in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Tennessee on March 24, 2000; Marquita Madison was added as a plaintiff on September 14, 2000, and the United States intervened on November 28, 2000. The plaintiffs and DOJ alleged race-based student-on-student harassment at Sullivan East High School and deliberate indifference by school officials, asserting Title VI and Fourteenth Amendment/Equal Protection claims; the docket listed a $2.5 million demand. The defendants denied wrongdoing, but the case settled: on October 16, 2002, the court entered a consent judgment requiring SCBE for four years to adopt anti-harassment policies, retain experts, conduct training, keep incident records, and report to DOJ. The Madison plaintiffs separately received compensation under a confidential settlement, and the remaining claims against SCBE, Glover, and Peters were dismissed with prejudice on October 17, 2002.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core parties, racial-harassment deliberate-indifference claims, DOJ intervention, and consent-order settlement, but it adds many details not in the reference and slightly diverges on the dismissal timing.

cases.case_name: Beal v. Midlothian Independent School District
cases.reference: Parents of female  students brought suit against the school district, alleging unequal treatment for female athletes and inadequate accommodations in violation of Title IX of the Education Amendment and the Equal Protection Clause. After mediation, the parties agreed to a joint stipulation. The court entered a consent decree in which the school district agreed to improve facilities for female athletics and increase the publicity of athletics offered for both males and females. The case was dismissed on January 22, 2003.
cases.summary: On April 18, 2001, parents of Amber Neely, Allison Maloney, Katie Maloney, and Valerie Kay Emmett filed a putative class action in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas against Midlothian Independent School District, Superintendent Molly Helmlinger in her official capacity, and Does 1–50. They alleged Title IX and Equal Protection/§ 1983 violations based on unequal participation opportunities and unequal treatment and benefits for female student athletes in school-sponsored sports. On May 21, 2002, Judge Sam A. Lindsay dismissed the Title IX effective-accommodation claim for lack of standing but certified a class for unequal-treatment/benefits claims. The parties entered a consent decree on September 11, 2002 requiring gender-neutral athletics policies and specific softball-facility improvements, with no admission of liability; after a joint stipulation, the court dismissed all claims with prejudice on January 22, 2003.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the basic parties, Title IX/Equal Protection claims, consent decree, facility improvements, and dismissal date, but it adds several unsupported procedural specifics and omits the decree’s requirement to increase publicity for both male and female athletics.

cases.case_name: Halbert v. Kilgore Indep. Sch. Dist.
cases.reference: In 2000, mother of a female student filed suit against a school district, alleging that it had violated Title IX by denying her daughter access to cheerleading tryouts as a result of disciplinary action, while male students receiving the same disciplinary action were not barred from participating in athletics for the upcoming school year as a result of the punishment. The plaintiff also alleged that the school district violated Title IX by promoting and funding male athletic programs in a manner unequal to the funding and promotion provided for female programs. The parties reached a settlement during mediation and the case was dismissed on October 9, 2001. No settlement agreement was filed with the court.
cases.summary: Susie Halbert, as next friend of her minor daughter, sued Kilgore Independent School District in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Texas, Tyler Division, on December 8, 2000. The complaint alleged Title IX sex discrimination, claiming KISD barred her daughter from 2000 cheerleader tryouts after an alcohol-related disciplinary incident while allowing similarly punished male athletes to play soccer and baseball, and also provided unequal uniforms, equipment, and supplies to female athletic activities compared with football. On May 29, 2001, Halbert filed an amended complaint seeking class relief for similarly situated female students, plus damages, declaratory and injunctive relief, costs, and attorneys’ fees. The case settled at mediation on August 28, 2001, and Judge William M. Steger dismissed KISD with prejudice on October 9, 2001, with each party bearing its own costs.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core Title IX cheerleading/athletics funding claims and settlement dismissal, but adds many unsupported procedural and factual details and omits that no settlement agreement was filed with the court.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. PROVIDENCE HEALTH SYSTEM
cases.reference:
On June 14, 2006, the EEOC filed this ADEA case against Providence Health System in the United States District Court for the District of Alaska on behalf of employees who claimed to have been denied re-hire after lay-off due to their age. On June 3, 2009, the parties entered into a three year Consent Decree in which the Defendants promised to be fully compliant with ADEA, to implement a non-discrimination policy, and to pay the Plaintiffs $220,000.

According to the terms of the Consent Decree, this case was closed and the agreement terminated on June 3, 2012.
cases.summary: On June 14, 2006, the EEOC sued Providence Health System-Washington d/b/a Providence Alaska Medical Center in the U.S. District Court for the District of Alaska. The complaint alleged Providence violated the ADEA by failing to rehire Rebecca Petrie, Gola Anderson, Milagros Lopez, Lawrence Harris, and Canijie Sadiku after a 2005 layoff because they were over 40. Providence denied liability, but the case settled by a consent decree entered June 3, 2009. Providence agreed to pay $220,000 total to the claimants and accept three years of injunctive relief, including anti-discrimination policies, training, posting, and EEOC reporting; the docket was closed June 10, 2009.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core ADEA rehire-after-layoff claim, consent decree, three-year term, and $220,000 payment, but adds several unsupported specifics and conflicts with the reference by stating the docket closed in 2009 rather than noting termination/closure on June 3, 2012.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Zale Corporation
cases.reference: In this sex/pregnancy discrimination case, the EEOC settled with Zale Corporation on behalf of a female employee who claimed she was denied a promotion because she was pregnant.  The Decree covers all employees of all Zales Jewelers stores in the State of Alaska.  It prohibits sex/pregnancy discrimination and calls for training and evaluation of managers and supervisors to ensure they provide a workplace free of sex/pregnancy-based discrimination.  Te Decree requires EEO policy posting and distribution and expungement of the employee's file.  The employer paid the claimant $55,000.00 in damages and agreed to a two-year duration for the Decree.  It expired on July 9, 2009.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Zale Corporation in the U.S. District Court for the District of Alaska on March 15, 2007, alleging Title VII sex/pregnancy discrimination. The complaint alleged Zale failed to promote Christina Kantarzis to store manager at the Sears Mall Zales Jewelers location in Anchorage because she was pregnant, after she filed an EEOC charge on June 27, 2006. Zale denied liability, but the parties entered a consent decree dated July 3, 2007, requiring Zale to pay Kantarzis $55,000 less required deductions and to implement Alaska-region anti-discrimination training, policy distribution, record expungement, reporting, and notice posting for two years. On July 9, 2007, Judge John W. Sedwick approved the consent decree and dismissed the case with prejudice, with each side bearing its own fees and costs.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. AUTOZONE INC
cases.reference: In September 2006, the EEOC brought this suit against Autozone for discharging its employee who opposed discriminatory conduct of his supervisor on the basis of race and sex. The court dismissed the claim on 10/30/2007.
cases.summary: On September 29, 2006, the U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued AutoZone, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Alabama, Civil Action No. 1:06-cv-00607-KD-C. The EEOC alleged Title VII retaliation, claiming AutoZone fired Roderick Broughton on January 30, 2004, after he complained about race discrimination and sexual harassment at Mobile-area stores and participated in an internal harassment investigation. On October 30, 2007, Judge Kristi K. DuBose granted AutoZone’s motion for summary judgment, holding that Broughton had not engaged in protected activity under Title VII because there was no EEOC proceeding before his termination and his belief that the conduct violated Title VII was not objectively reasonable. Judgment was entered the same day dismissing the EEOC’s claims with prejudice, with each party bearing its own costs.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the EEOC suit against AutoZone, the retaliation/discriminatory-opposition basis, and the dismissal date, though it adds many specific details and legal reasoning not present in the reference.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Ryder / Ate
cases.reference:
On March 29, 2002, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission ("EEOC"), on behalf of a female employee (plaintiff-intervenor) at a corporation, filed a lawsuit in the Northern District Court of Alabama under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, as amended, against Ryder/ATE, Inc.  The EEOC sought injunctive relief, compensatory damages, punitive damages, attorneys' fees and costs, and other forms of affirmative relief claiming that the defendant, through the actions of one of its supervisors, was liable for sexual harassment.  

On October 10, 2002, the EEOC and plaintiff-intervenor filed an amended complaint.  On September 9, 2003, the District Court (Judge U.W. Clemon) issued a consent decree where the defendant had to pay the plaintiff-intervenor $85,000 and reinstate her in her previous position, with seniority.
cases.summary: On March 29, 2002, the EEOC sued Ryder/ATE, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Alabama, Western Division, under Title VII and the Civil Rights Act of 1991. The EEOC alleged that supervisor Alfonso Pollard sexually harassed employee Pamelia Smoot in 1998–1999, that Ryder/ATE failed to act promptly after reports, and that Smoot was forced into a lower-paying job and leave; Smoot intervened on July 23, 2002, adding retaliation and related Title VII claims. The defendant was later identified as First Transit, Inc., formerly known as Ryder/ATE, Inc. The case settled by consent decree entered September 9, 2003: First Transit denied liability but agreed through a separate agreement to pay Smoot $85,000, reinstate her as a fixed route operator with seniority effective August 18, 2003, and each party bore its own costs and attorneys’ fees.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core Title VII sexual harassment suit, parties, settlement amount, and reinstatement remedy, but adds many unverified specifics and omits some reference details such as the October 10 amended complaint and requested relief.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. House of Philadelphia Center, Inc.
cases.reference: Equal Employment Opportunity Commission brought a Title VII sex discrimination case against House of Philadelphia, Inc., on behalf of an employee who was allegedly fired because she was pregnant. The EEOC sought monetary and injunctive relief for the employee (including economic damage, compensation for emotional harm, and punitive damages). The employee later intervened in the suit. The parties came to a settlement that was entered as a consent decree. The decree provided for monetary relief for the employee and subjected House of Philadelphia to a 3-year injunction. The case is now closed.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued House of Philadelphia Center, Inc. on September 19, 2005, in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Alabama, alleging Title VII sex/pregnancy discrimination for discharging employee Sharonda Griffin on October 15, 2004 because she was pregnant. Griffin intervened on February 21, 2006, asserting Title VII pregnancy/sex discrimination, hostile work environment, and state-law defamation claims. The case resolved by a consent decree entered by Judge Kristi K. DuBose on January 9, 2007, with House of Philadelphia denying liability but agreeing to pay Griffin $8,000 in two $4,000 installments and to implement anti-discrimination policies, training, complaint procedures, and notices at its Alabama facilities for 36 months.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core EEOC pregnancy discrimination claim, intervention, consent decree, monetary relief, and 36-month injunction, but adds many unsupported specifics and slightly varies the defendant name while omitting that the case is closed.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. TYSON FOOD, INC.
cases.reference: In 2005, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission filed a lawsuit in the Northern District of Alabama against Tyson Foods, Inc. Fourteen current and former African American employees at Tyson's Ashland, Alabama plant filed a lawsuit over the same issues, and the cases were consolidated. Plaintiffs claimed that Tyson had maintained racially segregated facilities and had subjected African American employees to a racially hostile work environment and retaliation and threats. On November 7, 2006, the parties settled and Plaintiffs received $871,000, while Tyson was required to institute anti-discrimination policies and programs at its Ashland plant.
cases.summary: On August 11, 2005, the EEOC sued Tyson Foods, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Alabama, Eastern Division, alleging Title VII race discrimination and retaliation at Tyson’s Ashland, Alabama plant. The EEOC alleged Tyson maintained a locked, racially segregated bathroom marked “Whites Only” and “Out of Order,” with keys distributed to white employees, and retaliated against Henry Adams and Leon Walker after they complained; 13 African-American employees also sued/intervened asserting Title VII, § 1981, and Alabama-law claims for hostile work environment, retaliation, outrage, and negligent management. The cases were consolidated on November 1, 2005 as In re Tyson Foods Litigation, No. CV-05-BE-1704-E, and settled after mediation. On November 7, 2006, Judge Karon O. Bowdre entered a consent decree and final judgment: Tyson denied liability but agreed to pay $871,000 and comply with a three-year decree requiring anti-discrimination measures, a consent-decree coordinator, training, reporting, monitoring, and supervisor accountability.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and captures the parties, race discrimination/hostile environment/retaliation claims, consolidation, settlement amount, and injunctive relief, but it conflicts with the reference by stating 13 rather than 14 African-American employee plaintiffs and adds unverified extra details.

cases.case_name: Belue v. Wayne Farms, LLC
cases.reference: This case was brought by several then and former employees of the defendant againt the defendant Wayne Farms LLC., seeking damages.  The case was transferred to the Southern District of Mississippi.  (See EE-MS-0060).
cases.summary: Jimmy W. Belue, Lisa Blankenship, Paul Naylor, Michael Rice, and other current/former production employees sued Wayne Farms LLC on October 18, 2006, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Alabama, Middle Division. The amended FLSA collective-action complaint alleged Wayne Farms’ Albertville, Alabama chicken-processing plant failed to pay hourly employees regular and overtime wages for “off the clock” time, including donning/doffing protective gear, washing, walking, security checks, waiting, and unpaid breaks under a master-time system. The court entered a stipulated discovery/protective order on March 7, 2007, later granted notice to similarly situated first- and second-processing employees on July 3, 2007, and appointed James Harris as special master on August 3, 2007. On November 5, 2007, the JPML transferred this case and related Wayne Farms FLSA cases to the Southern District of Mississippi for consolidated MDL pretrial proceedings before Judge Keith Starrett; no merits ruling or settlement is reflected in this record.

cases.reference: The case was brought by 15 African-American employees of the Veterans Affairs Medical Center against defendant Secretary of the Veteran Affairs seeking declaratory judgment and injunctive relief.  On January 3, 2003, the Court granted defendant's motion for summary judgment, and dismissed the case accordingly.
cases.summary: Georgia Gray Hampton and 14 other African-American employees of the Birmingham Veterans Affairs Medical Center filed a putative class action on December 1, 2000, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia against the Secretary of Veterans Affairs; the case was transferred to the Northern District of Alabama on March 15, 2001. Plaintiffs alleged Title VII and §§ 1981/1981a race discrimination, hostile work environment, retaliation, and promotion/training discrimination, with an original $10 million demand; later amended claims sought $300,000 per plaintiff per violation. On October 30, 2001, Judge William M. Acker Jr. granted partial summary judgment, dismissing the class claims, § 1981 claims, and many individual claims for failure to exhaust or untimeliness, leaving only several individual Title VII/§ 1981a claims. On January 3, 2003, the court granted summary judgment for the Department based on deemed admissions under Rule 36(b) and an attempted fraud on the court; the Eleventh Circuit affirmed in 2004, and later post-judgment motions/appeals were denied or dismissed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the basic parties and January 3, 2003 summary-judgment outcome, but it omits the reference’s stated declaratory/injunctive relief and adds many unsupported details about claims, damages, procedural history, and appeals.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Catastrophe Management Solutions
cases.reference: In 2013 a black job applicant filed this lawsuit in the Southern District of Alabama after being denied employment by a prospective employer, Catastrophe Management Solutions. ("CMS") The plaintiff alleged that CMS's policy of refusing employment to people with dreadlocks was prohibited by Title VII as a form a racial discrimination. In 2014, the district court dismissed the case for failure to state a plausible claim of racial discrimination. That same year, the plaintiff appealed to the 11th Circuit. In 2016, the 11th Circuit affirmed the dismissal.
cases.summary: On September 30, 2013, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued Catastrophe Management Solutions in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Alabama. The EEOC brought Title VII race-discrimination claims on behalf of Chastity C. Jones, alleging CMS withdrew her job offer after she refused to cut off her dreadlocks under its grooming policy. On March 27, 2014, Senior Judge Charles R. Butler Jr. granted CMS’s motion to dismiss and entered judgment for CMS; the court later denied the EEOC’s motion to amend on June 2, 2014. The EEOC appealed, and the Eleventh Circuit affirmed in a revised published opinion on December 13, 2016; the mandate issued December 13, 2017, and the case is closed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the Title VII dreadlocks claim, CMS, dismissal, appeal, and affirmance clearly, but it diverges from the reference by identifying the EEOC rather than the applicant as the suing plaintiff and adds potentially extraneous date/procedural details.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Tanami Trading Corporation (d/b/a Left Lane Express)
cases.reference: In 2006 the EEOC filed this in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Arkansas. The plaintiffs alleged a racially hostile work environment and constructive discharge. In 2008, a consent decree granted individual and injunctive damages to the defendants. Its effective period was three years, and there's nothing more on the docket - so presumably the matter ended in 2011.
cases.summary: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued Tanami Trading Corporation d/b/a Left Lane Express in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Arkansas, Pine Bluff Division, on September 29, 2006. The EEOC alleged under Title VII and the Civil Rights Act of 1991 that Bobby Huggins, Carla Huggins, Charles Morehead, and other Black employees were subjected to a racially hostile work environment, and that Bobby and Carla Huggins were constructively discharged. The case was resolved by a consent decree entered June 13, 2008, with no admission of liability. Tanami agreed to pay $70,000 total—$25,000 each to Bobby and Carla Huggins and $20,000 to Charles Morehead—and to provide injunctive relief, training, notice posting, reporting, and neutral references.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and consistent with the reference while adding specific details, but it omits the decree’s three-year effective period and the presumed 2011 conclusion.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Tobacco Superstores, Inc.
cases.reference: On September 19, 2005, an African American employee, on behalf of other African American employees, filed this lawsuit against Tobacco Superstores Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Arkansas. The plaintiff, represented by the EEOC, alleged that she and other were denied promotions because of their race. She also alleged that Tobacco Superstores Inc. constructively discharged her because of her race. In 2008, District Judge William R. Wilson approved a consent decree granting injunctive and monetary relief. The decree's effective period was three years, and there is nothing more on the docket - so presumably the matter ended in 2011.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Tobacco Superstores, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Arkansas, Jonesboro Division, on September 19, 2005. The complaint alleged Title VII race discrimination, claiming Tobacco Superstores failed to promote Theresa Sharkey and other African American employees to management positions and constructively discharged Sharkey. On June 6, 2008, the court granted partial summary judgment limiting claims before September 28, 2001 and dismissing class member Solita Brown’s piggybacking claim, but denied summary judgment on the forward temporal scope and judicial-estoppel defenses. The case settled: a consent decree entered August 4, 2008 required Tobacco Superstores to pay $425,000, implement promotion and nondiscrimination policies, provide race-discrimination training, report to the EEOC for three years, and refrain from race discrimination and retaliation, without admitting liability.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the central allegations, parties, consent decree relief, and three-year term clearly, though it frames the EEOC as the suing party rather than the employee and adds several specific procedural and monetary details not present in the reference.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. BFI Waste Services, LLC d/b/a/ Allied Waste Services of Little Rock
cases.reference: This sex discrimination case was brought by the EEOC in District Court in Arkansas on behalf of female employees who were being refused jobs as drivers for BFI Waste Services on the basis of their sex.  The case settled quickly with a three-year Consent Decree prohibiting the empoyer's refusal to hire female drivers, prohibiting retaliation, requiring non-discrimination training,  open position posting, EEO policy posting, reporting, and $190,780 in damages to the claimants.  The Decree expires on March 27, 2011.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued BFI Waste Services, LLC d/b/a Allied Waste Services of Little Rock in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Arkansas on September 28, 2007. The complaint alleged Title VII sex discrimination, claiming BFI denied Sherri/Sherry Morehead, Dawn Gaddy, and other female applicants opportunities to apply for or be hired as truck drivers because they were women. BFI denied liability, but the case resolved by consent decree entered March 27, 2008. Under the decree, BFI agreed to three years of injunctive relief, training, posting, and reporting, and to pay $85,245.09 to Morehead, $75,253.48 to Gaddy, and $30,281.49 to class member Karen Coleman; the case was terminated.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is factually consistent with the reference and clearly written, capturing the parties, sex-discrimination hiring claims, consent decree, relief, and damages, though it omits some decree terms such as anti-retaliation and specific posting requirements.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Family Dollar Stores of Arkansas, Inc.
cases.reference: This race discrimination case was brought by the EEOC against Family Dollar Stores of Arkansas on behalf of a black former employee who claimed she was denied promotion and constructively discharged from her job based on her race.  With almost no litigation, the case was settled with a three-year Consent Decree which requires the employer to stop denying promotions on the basis of race, to conduct non-discrimination training, post policies and not to retaliate against any employee or applicant.  The employer agreed to pay $25,000.00 in damages to the claimant.   This Decree is in effect until June 30, 2011; three years from its entry.
cases.summary: On June 29, 2007, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued Family Dollar Stores of Arkansas, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Arkansas, Western Division. The EEOC alleged under Title VII and the Civil Rights Act of 1991 that Family Dollar failed to promote Debra Terry, an African American employee, to store manager positions because of race and constructively discharged her. Family Dollar denied liability, but the parties resolved the case by a consent decree entered June 30, 2008. Under the decree, Family Dollar agreed to pay Terry $4,279.59 in back pay and $20,720.41 in damages, provide anti-discrimination training and notices, report promotion-related race discrimination complaints, expunge references to the charge/litigation from Terry’s personnel file, and give neutral references; the case was terminated.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is factually consistent with the reference and clearly written, capturing the parties, claims, settlement, and payment, though it omits the decree’s three-year duration and some injunctive terms such as non-retaliation.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Bashas' Inc.
cases.reference: On August 17, 2005, non-Navajo Native Americans filed this lawsuit in the United States District Court for the District of Arizona. The plaintiffs, represented by the EEOC alleged that Bashas’ Inc. engaged in unlawful employment practices by discriminating against the plaintiffs on the basis of their national origin. On December 14, 2006, District Judge Stephen M. McNamee ordered a stay on the proceedings pending the appeals in the EEOC v Peabody Western Coal case. On September 26, 2014 the Peabody case was decided against the EEOC. On November 12, 2014 Judge Stephen M. McNamee dismissed with prejudice the case.
cases.summary: On August 17, 2005, the EEOC sued Bashas’ Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona, naming the Navajo Nation as a Rule 19 defendant. The EEOC alleged Title VII national-origin discrimination, claiming Bashas refused to hire qualified non-Navajo Native Americans, including Hopi charging parties Dale Lucero and Randy Honahni, for jobs at its Tuba City supermarket, and also alleged Title VII recordkeeping violations. The case was repeatedly stayed while related EEOC v. Peabody Western Coal litigation proceeded. On November 10, 2014, the court entered the parties’ stipulated dismissal with prejudice of all claims, with each party bearing its own costs and attorneys’ fees.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the basic parties, national-origin discrimination claim, stay pending Peabody, and dismissal with prejudice, but it adds several unsupported details and gives a dismissal date that conflicts with the reference while omitting the Peabody outcome and some procedural specifics.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Eagle Produce
cases.reference: In 2006, the EEOC filed this case against Eagle Produce in the U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona. The EEOC alleged sex and wage discrimination. In 2008 the parties reached a settlement agreement which included monetary compensation, as well as requirements for training and reporting.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Eagle Produce, L.L.C. in the U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona on August 7, 2006, later amending the complaint to assert Equal Pay Act and Title VII claims on behalf of Anita Guerrero. The EEOC alleged Eagle paid Guerrero, a female quality control inspector at its Arizona facilities, less than male employees performing substantially equal work and otherwise discriminated against her because of sex. On June 5, 2008, the court granted summary judgment to Eagle on the Title VII claim and on Equal Pay Act claims before August 7, 2004, but allowed the Equal Pay Act claim from August 7, 2004 forward to proceed; reconsideration was denied July 18, 2008. The case settled and was dismissed with prejudice on October 3, 2008, with Eagle agreeing to pay Guerrero $9,000 total ($3,000 back pay, $3,000 liquidated damages, and $3,000 compensatory damages) and to comply with notice, policy, training, and reporting obligations for 12 months.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is consistent with the reference and captures the filing, parties, discrimination claims, settlement outcome, monetary relief, and training/reporting obligations in clear professional prose.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Lumpy (d/b/a Chilly Bombers Bar & Grill)
cases.reference: In March 2006, the Phoenix District Office of the EEOC filed this lawsuit against Lumpy, LLC, doing business as Chilly Bombers Bar & Grill, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona alleging discrimination on the basis of gender, female and pregnancy in violation of the Equal Pay Act of 1963 and Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. A jury found in favor of the plaintiff and Judge Susan Bolton ordered the defendant to pay $200.00 in damages.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Lumpy LLC, d/b/a Chilly Bombers Bar & Grill, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona on March 22, 2006, alleging Title VII sex/pregnancy discrimination and Equal Pay Act violations involving manager Christine Sedita. The EEOC claimed Lumpy refused to put Sedita on salary and paid her less than male managers, including Jeff Majerle and Patrick Zacek, because she was female and pregnant. On February 1, 2008, the court granted summary judgment only as to the EPA claim comparing Sedita to Majerle, but allowed the Title VII claim and EPA claim comparing Sedita to Zacek to proceed. After a March 2008 jury trial, the jury found for the EEOC on pregnancy discrimination and the Equal Pay Act claim, awarded $200 in pay-differential damages, and awarded no Title VII damages or punitive damages; final judgment entered May 7, 2008, requiring Lumpy to pay $200 plus interest from August 14, 2004, while denying broader injunctive relief and back pay.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the parties, court, discrimination statutes, general claims, and $200 plaintiff victory, though it adds many case-specific details not present in the reference and omits Judge Susan Bolton's name.

cases.case_name: Hendrix v. Chipotle Mexican Grill Incorporated
cases.reference: In 2020, the plaintiff filed this lawsuit against her employer, Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc., in the U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona. The plaintiff alleged that the defendant discriminated against her due to her sex by refusing to allow her to take a break during her shift to pump breastmilk. The case is ongoing.
cases.summary: Noel Hendrix sued Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona on August 12, 2020, initially as a putative collective action, and filed a First Amended Verified Complaint on September 1, 2020 as an individual plaintiff. She alleges Title VII/Pregnancy Discrimination Act sex/pregnancy discrimination based on Chipotle managers allegedly denying her break time to express breast milk at a Glendale, Arizona restaurant in December 2016 and harassing her about needing pump breaks. The complaint states the EEOC issued a reasonable-cause determination finding Chipotle violated Title VII by preventing Hendrix from expressing breast milk at work and subjecting her to hostility and harassment. As of the docket provided, Chipotle was served with the amended complaint on September 3–4, 2020, and no ruling, settlement, or dismissal is reflected.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately includes the plaintiff, defendant, court, sex/pregnancy discrimination theory based on denial of breast-pumping breaks, and ongoing status, while adding consistent procedural detail in clear professional prose.

cases.case_name: EEOC (Hadji) v. Albion River Inn, Inc.
cases.reference:
The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) filed a race-or-national-origin retaliation and discrimination lawsuit against Albion River Inn, Inc., on behalf of an Arab restaurant manager who was fired for refusing to apologize to a customer he asked to leave the restaurant. The customer allegedly used racial epithets, slurs, and violent language toward the manager and a Tunisian employee under him. The EEOC sought monetary and injunctive relief for the employee and also sought its costs.The employee intervened in the case adding state-law claims and seeking monetary relief.

The parties eventually did decide to settle, agreed to settle on February 4, 2008, the first day of trial. The Court later ordered the parties to pay the costs of empaneling a jury for the first day of trial. The Court (Judge Susan Illston) entered their 3-year settlement agreement as a consent decree. The decree contained both monetary and injunctive relief. The employee was to be paid $165,000, inclusive of $75,000 for attorneys' fees and $15,000 for costs. No further Court involvement appears on the docket, and the case is now closed.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Albion River Inn, Inc. on August 31, 2006, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging Title VII race/national-origin discrimination and retaliation involving Abdellatif Hadji, an Arab/Moroccan restaurant manager. The EEOC alleged Albion fired Hadji on November 18, 2004, after he opposed race/national-origin harassment by a diner toward a Tunisian waiter and toward Hadji himself; Hadji intervened on February 9, 2007, adding Title VII, FEHA, intentional-infliction, and wrongful-termination claims. The case settled before trial on February 4, 2008, and Judge Susan Illston entered a consent decree on February 27, 2008, requiring Albion to pay $165,000: $75,000 to Hadji for emotional distress, $75,000 in attorneys’ fees, and $15,000 in costs. The decree also required revised anti-discrimination policies, complaint procedures, employee training, posting, and supervisor-accountability measures for three years, without admission of liability; Hadji’s complaint in intervention was dismissed on July 8, 2008.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main parties, claims, settlement, consent decree, and monetary relief, but includes several unsupported extra details and slightly conflicts with the reference by saying the case settled before trial rather than on the first day of trial, while omitting the jury-cost order a

cases.case_name: EEOC v. AMELCO ELECTRIC COMPANY, INC.
cases.reference: In June 2005, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission filed this suit in the Northern District of California under Title VII against Amelco Electric S.F., Inc. and Amelco Corporation, claiming Amelco had discriminated against its employees because of the sex, race, national origin, and religion. The parties settled on August 28, 2006, and plaintiffs were awarded $125,000.
cases.summary: On June 20, 2005, the EEOC sued Amelco Electric San Francisco and Amelco Corporation in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, Case No. 3:05-cv-02492-MEJ. The complaint alleged Title VII violations on behalf of Carl McNair and similarly situated employees, including hostile work environment, disparate treatment and/or termination based on race, national origin, sex, and religion, and retaliation against McNair for discrimination complaints. The parties settled this case and related Case No. 06-3008 CRB for a total of $125,000, with the EEOC to allocate payments among McNair, Susanna Ortez, and class members. On August 21, 2006, Magistrate Judge Maria-Elena James dismissed the case with prejudice and closed the file.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main parties, Title VII discrimination claims, venue, settlement, and $125,000 amount, but it adds unsupported details and gives a dismissal/settlement timing that conflicts with the reference’s August 28, 2006 settlement date.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. AMELCO ELECTRIC SAN FRANCISCO, INC.
cases.reference: In May 2006, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission filed this suit in the Northern District of California under Title VII against Amelco Electric S.F., Inc. and Amelco Corporation, claiming Amelco had discriminated against its employees because of the sex, race, and/or national origin. The parties settled on August 28, 2006, and plaintiffs were awarded $125,000.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Amelco Electric San Francisco and Amelco Corporation in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California on May 4, 2006 (Case No. 3:06-cv-03008-CRB). The complaint alleged Title VII violations on behalf of Susanna Ortez and similarly situated electricians, claiming hostile work environment, harassment, disparate treatment, and/or termination based on sex, race, and national origin at the San Francisco worksite since at least January 2002. The parties reached a tentative settlement on July 14, 2006, and a signed settlement agreement filed August 28, 2006 resolved this case and related Case No. 05-02492 MEJ for a total of $125,000, with no admission of liability and a non-retaliation provision. The EEOC filed a voluntary dismissal with prejudice on August 21, 2006, and the case was closed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core parties, court, Title VII discrimination allegations, settlement timing, and $125,000 outcome, but it adds numerous specific details not present in the reference, including named claimants, related case information, and procedural terms.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Robert G. Aptekar, M.D. (d/b/a Arthritis and Orthopedic Medical Clinic)
cases.reference:
On August 9, 2006, the San Francisco office of the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission filed a lawsuit under Title VII against Robert G. Aptekar, MD, Inc. and the Arthritis and Orthopedic Medical Clinic in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California.  The EEOC alleged that the defendants had violated the rights of the complainant by subjecting her to racial harassment, subjecting her to a hostile work environment, and by retaliating against her when she filed a complaint with the EEOC.  

On July 16, 2007, the parties entered a consent decree set to last 18 months, which the District Court (Judge Ronald M. Whyte) approved on August 20, 2007.  Among other things, the defendants agreed to pay the complainant $44,000 in unspecified damages.
cases.summary: On August 9, 2006, the EEOC sued Robert G. Aptekar, M.D., Inc., d/b/a Arthritis and Orthopedic Medical Clinic, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. The EEOC alleged Title VII race discrimination and retaliation, claiming Tomeika L. Broussard, an African-American employee, was subjected to a racially hostile work environment and discharged for complaining about harassment. The defendant denied wrongdoing, but the case fully settled in mediation on February 12, 2007. On August 20, 2007, Judge Ronald M. Whyte entered a consent decree requiring the defendant to pay Broussard $44,000 and implement anti-harassment, anti-retaliation, training, and reporting measures for 18 months; the civil case was terminated.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main lawsuit, Title VII harassment/retaliation claims, consent decree, judge, duration, and $44,000 payment, but adds several unsupported details such as the complainant’s name/race, discharge, mediation settlement date, denial of wrongdoing, and specific remedial measures.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Borland Software Corporation
cases.reference: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) filed this lawsuit against Borland Software Corporation on behalf of a female Vietnamese employee who was being paid less than her white male counterparts.The parties had been participating in settlement negotiations and reached settlement shortly after the case was filed. The 3-year consent decree provided monetary and injunctive relief, including anti-discrimination, anti-retaliation, and training provisions.Borland was required to pay the employee $66,500 for backpay damages and $13,500 for uncovered expenses. The employee's salary was raised from $100,000 to $110,000. No further court involvement appears on the docket and the case is now closed.
cases.summary: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued Borland Software Corporation on September 30, 2005, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging violations of Title VII, the Equal Pay Act, and the Civil Rights Act of 1991. The EEOC alleged Borland paid Tuyen Tran, an Asian Vietnamese female employee performing Director of Technical Support duties in Cupertino, less than white male employees doing substantially similar work and otherwise discriminated against her based on sex, race, and national origin. Borland denied liability, but the case was resolved by a consent decree entered by Magistrate Judge Howard R. Lloyd on October 19, 2005. Under the three-year decree, Borland agreed to pay Tran $80,000 total ($66,500 backpay and $13,500 expense reimbursement), raise her annual salary from $100,000 to $110,000, update her personnel file to reflect a Director title effective April 2002, provide specified EEO/diversity training, and refrain from discrimination or retaliation.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the EEOC suit, unequal pay allegations, consent decree, monetary relief, salary increase, and anti-discrimination/training provisions, though it adds several specific details not in the reference and omits the docket closure point.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Foam Works, LLC
cases.reference: On September 27, 2006, The EEOC filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Easter District of California. The plaintiff sued Foam Works, LLC under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 alleging sexual harassment and a hostile work environment. On October 12, 2007, District Judge Morrison C. England approved and ordered a proposed consent decree. The consent decree required the Defendant to pay the complainant $21,500 in compensatory damages. The Defendant was also required to conduct annual training on harassment, institute a confidential complaint procedure, retain pertinent records, and allow the EEOC to inspect documents with twenty days notice.
cases.summary: The U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued Foam Works, LLC in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of California on September 27, 2006. The EEOC brought Title VII claims alleging that Foam Works subjected charging party Melodie Clark to sex-based harassment/hostile work environment and retaliated against her after she complained, including reduced duties, reduced wages, and discharge. Foam Works answered, and the case settled before the VDRP process. On October 12, 2007, Judge Morrison C. England Jr. approved a three-year consent decree requiring Foam Works to pay Clark $21,500 in compensatory damages, comply with anti-discrimination and anti-retaliation obligations, revise policies, train employees, report to the EEOC, and post notice, with no admission of liability.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Hometown Buffet
cases.reference: On September 29, 2006, the Los Angeles District Office of the EEOC filed this suit in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of California. The plaintiffs sued Hometown Buffet Inc. under for violation of Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Specifically, the plaintiffs alleged sexual harassment and a hostile work environment. On July 22, 2009, Judge Michael M. Anello approved the proposed consent decree put forth by the parties. Its effective period was two years, and there is nothing more on the docket - so presumably the matter ended in 2011.
cases.summary: The U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued Hometown Buffet, Inc. and Does 1–10 in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of California on September 29, 2006. The EEOC alleged Title VII sex discrimination, claiming female employees at Hometown Buffet’s El Cajon, California restaurant—including Yesica Owen and Eliza Navarette—were subjected to sexual harassment/hostile work environment and that the company failed to take effective corrective action. On March 6, 2007, the court denied Hometown Buffet’s motion challenging the EEOC’s pre-suit conciliation and granted the EEOC’s cross-motion finding conciliation satisfied. After Hometown Buffet entered Chapter 11 proceedings, the case resolved by a July 22, 2009 consent decree providing allowed bankruptcy claims of $25,000 each for several claimants, $35,000 plus counseling for Julia Lopez, a $500,000 EEOC allowed claim allocated among class members, and two years of injunctive relief/training; the case was closed that day.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the parties, Title VII sexual-harassment/hostile-environment claims, consent decree date, and two-year relief period clearly, though it adds many details not in the reference and somewhat conflicts with the reference’s implication that the matter ended after the decree period ra

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Interstate Hotels, LLC
cases.reference: The EEOC sued Interstate Hotels, LLC in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California in September 2008, alleging Title VII violations. Following mediation, discovery, and a motion for summary judgment by the defendant, the parties settled the lawsuit in October 2005 through a consent decree, which was set to last 30 months. Since there is no subsequent enforcement activity, presumably the matter closed finally in April 2008.
cases.summary: On September 28, 2004, the EEOC sued Interstate Hotels, L.L.C. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging Title VII national-origin harassment and retaliation at the San Francisco Marriott at Fisherman’s Wharf on behalf of Raul Gutierrez and similarly situated Latino/Hispanic employees. Gutierrez intervened individually, adding claims and defendants HMC Acquisition Properties, Inc., John Trovato, and Penny Richardson. The court struck several affirmative defenses on April 14, 2005, and denied defendants’ June 23, 2005 summary-judgment motion against the EEOC. The case resolved by an October 5, 2005 consent decree in which Interstate denied liability but agreed to pay $320,000, redistribute anti-discrimination policies, provide annual training, and report complaints for 30 months; Gutierrez’s intervention was dismissed on November 25, 2005.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and captures the main parties, Title VII claims, summary-judgment activity, consent decree, and 30-month term, but it conflicts with the reference on the filing date and omits the presumed final closure in April 2008.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Paragary's Management Group
cases.reference: On September 29, 2005 the San Francisco District Office of the EEOC filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of California. The plaintiff sued Paragary's Management Group, Paramoor, Inc., and PDK Partnership under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. The EEOC represented claimants who alleged the defendants had subjected them to a hostile work environment and disparate treatment. On April 29, 2009 Judge Morrison C. England approved and ordered a consent decree to be in effect for three years. The decree granted permanent injunctive relief and $60,000 in monetary damages. There is nothing more in the docket, so presumably the matter ended in 2012.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Paragary’s Management Group, Paramoor, Inc., PDK Partnership, and PK Partnership in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of California, Sacramento Division, filing the case on September 29, 2005 and amending the complaint on November 17, 2005. The EEOC alleged Title VII violations based on sex harassment/hostile work environment and sex and national-origin discrimination against Leticia Fernandez and similarly situated Mexican female employees at defendants’ Sacramento County restaurants. On April 28, 2009, Judge Morrison C. England Jr. entered a consent decree resolving all EEOC claims against the named defendants, with no admission of liability by Paramoor. Paramoor agreed to pay $60,000 in damages to be allocated by the EEOC between Fernandez and Juana Jimenez, implement anti-harassment policies and training, provide reference letters, and comply with reporting obligations for a three-year decree term; the case was closed on April 29, 2009.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main lawsuit, Title VII claims, consent decree, $60,000 payment, and three-year term, but it adds unverified or inconsistent details such as an extra defendant, different decree date/closure characterization, and specific claimant/discrimination details not in the reference.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Pizzeria Uno Lombard, Inc.
cases.reference: In December 2005, the EEOC district office in San Francisco, California brought this action against Pizzeria Uno Lombard, Inc., a regional restaurant chain, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California The EEOC alleged that the defendant violated Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 when it subjected female employees to a sexually hostile work environment.  The defendant never answered the complaint, and an entry of default was entered against it on September 8th, 2006. The EEOC voluntarily dismissed the action in April 2007 before the magistrate judge's report and recommendation for judgement was entered.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Pizzeria Uno Lombard, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California on December 14, 2005. The complaint alleged Title VII sex discrimination, claiming Hilda Betancourth and similarly situated female employees at the San Francisco restaurant were subjected to sexual harassment creating a hostile work environment since at least November 2002. The EEOC sought injunctive relief, policy changes, compensatory damages, punitive damages, and costs, with amounts to be determined at trial. After Pizzeria Uno was served on June 21, 2006 and failed to answer, default was entered on September 8, 2006, but the EEOC voluntarily dismissed the case without prejudice on April 18, 2007.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core parties, Title VII hostile work environment allegations, default, and voluntary dismissal, though it adds several details not in the reference and omits the note that dismissal occurred before the magistrate judge’s report and recommendation.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. TRIMBCO INC (d/b/a SHARP PRECISION MANUFACTURING)
cases.reference: In September 2006, the San Francisco District Office of the EEOC filed this lawsuit against Trimbco, Inc., doing business as Sharp Precision Manufacturing, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging discrimination on the basis of gender, female, and national origin, Hispanic. The case ended with a default judgment against defendants entered on March 17, 2009. The judgment required Defendant to pay $50,000 in compensatory and punitive damages to each of the six complainants. Additionally, the judgment enjoined defendants from further engaging in discrimination on the basis of sex or national origin, required defendants to institute policies and programs to ensure equal opportunities for employees.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Trimbco, Inc., d/b/a Sharp Precision Manufacturing, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California on September 29, 2006, on behalf of Anna Valdez McAllister and similarly situated employees. The complaint alleged Title VII sex and national-origin discrimination, claiming Trimbco subjected female and/or Hispanic employees to a hostile work environment at its San Jose facility. After Trimbco’s counsel withdrew and the corporation failed to obtain new counsel or oppose the EEOC’s motion, Magistrate Judge Patricia V. Trumbull granted default judgment on March 17, 2009. The court awarded $50,000 each to Liliana Aranda, Debra Garza, Laura Jennings, Leilani Martinez, Anna McAllister, and Melinda Safford, totaling $300,000, and enjoined Trimbco from sex- or national-origin-based harassment and discrimination.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the parties, claims, default judgment, damages, and injunction, but includes several unsupported extra details and omits the requirement that defendants institute equal-opportunity policies and programs.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. SIZZLER USA RESTAURANTS, INC
cases.reference: In 2006, the EEOC filed this case against Sizzler USA Restaurants, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. The EEOC alleged sex and national origin discrimination. In 2008 the parties reached a settlement agreement which included monetary compensation, as well as requirements for training and reporting.
cases.summary: On September 29, 2006, the EEOC sued Sizzler USA Restaurants, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, San Jose Division, under Title VII and the Civil Rights Act of 1991. The EEOC alleged that Sizzler subjected former Redwood City employee Patricia Huizache, a Mexican female, to a hostile work environment based on sex and national origin, leading to her constructive discharge on October 16, 2004; Huizache later intervened with parallel state claims. After discovery disputes and an unsuccessful mediation, the case resolved by consent decree entered August 28, 2008. Sizzler denied liability but agreed to pay Huizache $300,000 and to comply for five years with injunctive relief including anti-harassment policies, training, posting, reporting, and anti-retaliation requirements; the docket shows the case closed that day.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core parties, discrimination claims, 2008 settlement, and monetary/training/reporting relief accurately, though it adds many specific details not present in the reference.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. TAYLOR MADE DIGITAL SYTEMS, INC.
cases.reference:
In September 2005, the EEOC filed suit in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California under Title I and Title VII against Taylor Made Digital Systems. The EEOC sought injunctive relief and damages, alleging that the defendant discriminated against one of its employees on the basis of her sex (female/pregnant) and race (African American). 

In October 2006, the court  (Judge Maxine M. Chesney) signed a proposed consent decree. The consent decree required the defendant to pay $30,600 to the employee who brought the initial charge. The defendant also agreed to comply with Title VII, not to engage in retaliation against any employee because of the lawsuit, and to continue to post an anti-discrimination policy on its company intranet. Finally, the consent decree required all inquiries about the employee who filed the initial charge to be directed to a specific member of human resources, who was prohibited from referring to the present lawsuit. The consent decree terminated after one year.
cases.summary: On September 29, 2005, the EEOC sued Taylor Made Digital Systems, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging Title VII, Civil Rights Act of 1991, and Pregnancy Discrimination Act violations. The EEOC claimed Taylor Made discriminated against Chene Gasper, an African-American woman, based on race and sex/pregnancy by refusing to return her to her prior position after pregnancy, replacing and demoting her, and laying her off. On December 21, 2005, the EEOC amended the complaint to add Ricoh Corporation d/b/a Ricoh Business Systems, Inc., alleging Ricoh was Taylor Made’s successor after purchasing it around March 1, 2005. The case resolved by consent decree entered October 18, 2006, with no admission of liability; Ricoh agreed to pay Gasper $30,600 and maintain anti-discrimination and anti-retaliation policies, and the case was closed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the basic lawsuit, discrimination claims, consent decree date, payment amount, and policy obligations, but it adds several facts not in the reference and omits important decree terms such as the HR inquiry restriction, intranet posting, and one-year duration.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Children's Hospital and Research Center
cases.reference: In December 2013, the EEOC brought this suit against Children's Hospital and Research Center for not providing a reasonable accommodation and discharging its employee who got breast cancer and required treatment and recovery. In February 2015, the District Court granted approval of a joint proposed consent decree that provided for general and specific injunctive relief, as well as $300,000 for the plaintiff.
cases.summary: On December 11, 2013, the U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued Children’s Hospital and Research Center in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. The EEOC alleged under Title I of the ADA that the hospital failed to reasonably accommodate office associate Imelda Tamayo’s breast-cancer treatment leave and fired her on or about July 10, 2012, rather than extending leave or considering reassignment. On February 10, 2015, Judge Edward M. Chen entered a consent decree resolving the case without an admission of liability. The decree required Children’s Hospital to pay Tamayo $300,000 and imposed three years of ADA-related policy revisions, employee and manager training, notice posting, reporting, and neutral-reference/records relief.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately and clearly captures the EEOC’s ADA accommodation/discharge claims, the breast-cancer treatment context, the consent decree outcome, injunctive relief, and the $300,000 payment, with no apparent contradictions.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. McDonald's Restaurants of California, Inc.
cases.reference: On December 18, 2013, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, on behalf of a Muslim crew person, filed a lawsuit in the Eastern District Court of California under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and Title I of the Civil Rights Act of 1991 against McDonald's Corporation.  The EEOC alleged that the defendant discriminated against one if its employees based on his religion, Islam, when it refused to accommodate the employee's religious belief to wear a beard at work, resulting in the employee's constructive discharge. On December 19, 2013, the District Court issued a consent decree for two years where, among other things, the defendant agreed to pay the employee $50,000.
cases.summary: The U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission sued McDonald’s Restaurants of California, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of California, Case No. 1:13-cv-02065-AWI-SAB, filing an amended complaint on December 19, 2013. The EEOC alleged Title VII religious discrimination on behalf of Shaheed Khan, a Muslim employee at a Fresno McDonald’s, claiming McDonald’s failed to accommodate his request to wear a beard for religious reasons and constructively discharged him. The parties resolved the case by a consent decree approved December 20, 2013, requiring McDonald’s to pay Khan $50,000, purge certain negative employment records, and implement anti-discrimination, accommodation, training, posting, and reporting measures for two years. Judgment was entered and the case was closed on March 11, 2014.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Home Depot U.S.A., Inc.
cases.reference: On September 29, 2006, the Denver Office of the EEOC filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado. The plaintiffs sued Home Depot U.S.A. Inc. under Title VII. The plaintiff claimed that Home Depot retaliated against the former employee when it terminated her employment five weeks after it issued her a settlement check for a previous suit resolving charges of discrimination. On July 29, 2009, Judge Lewis T. Babcock approved a settlement agreement. The agreement included injunctive relief enjoining the defendant from retaliating against employees who complain about violations of Title VII and monetary relief of $84,750. The agreement's effective period was two years, and there is nothing further in the docket - so presumably the matter ended in 2011.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Home Depot U.S.A. Inc. d/b/a The Home Depot in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado on September 29, 2006, alleging Title VII retaliation on behalf of former employee Connie Hainline. The EEOC claimed Home Depot suspended Hainline on November 24, 2004 and terminated her effective December 3, 2004 because she had filed and resolved an earlier EEOC discrimination charge. On July 11, 2008, Judge Lewis T. Babcock denied Home Depot’s motion for summary judgment, finding triable issues on causation and pretext. The case settled, and on July 29, 2009 the court approved a settlement agreement and dismissed the action with prejudice; Home Depot agreed to pay Hainline $84,750, including $19,750 for past lost wages and $65,000 for compensatory damages, and to provide anti-retaliation policy, posting, training, and reporting relief for two years.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the EEOC Title VII retaliation suit, Home Depot as defendant, settlement approval, monetary relief, and two-year injunctive relief, though it adds several details not in the reference and omits the specific five-week-after-settlement-check framing and presumed 2011 en

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Moreland Auto Group, LLP
cases.reference: On September 25, 2006, the Denver Field Office and the Phoenix District Office of the EEOC filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado. The EEOC sued Moreland Auto Group, LLP under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Two former female employees sought injunctive relief to enjoin the defendant from discriminating against employees based on gender or from retaliating against employees who complain about discrimination. On August 22, 2008 Judge Edward J. Nottingham approved a settlement agreement that included a $70,000 fund to be dispersed amongst the claimants, and an injunction enjoining the defendants from gender-based discrimination.
cases.summary: On September 25, 2006, the EEOC sued Moreland Auto Group, LLP d/b/a Kids Automotive, Inc., Kids Financial, Inc., and Brandon Financial, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado, alleging Title VII sex discrimination, sexual harassment/hostile work environment, and retaliation. The EEOC alleged that female employees, including Georgene Wayne and Nancy Castanon, were subjected to pornography, lewd comments and gestures, and other sexual conduct at Denver and Aurora locations, and that Wayne and Castanon were scrutinized, demoted, and terminated after complaining. Wayne and Castanon intervened, but their intervention claims were dismissed with prejudice on October 9, 2007, and the settlement agreement later stated their claims had been settled separately. After the court denied defendants’ summary judgment motion and the EEOC’s motion to enforce an earlier settlement on January 29, 2008, the parties settled; on August 22, 2008, the court approved a three-year settlement requiring defendants to pay $70,000, maintain anti-discrimination policies, provide annual training, and dismissing the action with prejudice with each side bearing its own fees and costs.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the filing, defendant, Title VII gender/retaliation claims, settlement date, $70,000 payment, and injunctive relief, though it adds many procedural and factual details not in the reference and omits the EEOC office names and judge.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Benni's, LLC (d/b/a Bennigan's)
cases.reference: On August 17, 2006 the Boston Area Office and New York District Office of the EEOC filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of Connecticut. The plaintiffs sued Benni's LLC under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act. The plaintiff claimed that the defendant subjected the complaining parties to a sexually hostile work environment, leading to one employee's constructive discharge. On March 28, 2008, Judge Warren W. Eginton granted a motion for default judgment. The default judgment damages included injunctive relief in the form of postings, anti-discriminatory policies, trainings, and monitoring. It also required the defendant to pay a total of $190,000 to the two employees and to pay their attorney fees.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Benni’s, LLC d/b/a Bennigan’s in the U.S. District Court for the District of Connecticut on August 17, 2006, and Stefanie Horowitz intervened as a plaintiff. The complaint alleged Title VII sex discrimination at Bennigan’s Stamford location, including sexual harassment/hostile work environment, constructive discharge of Horowitz, and failure to preserve personnel records. After Benni’s counsel withdrew and the company failed to appear through new counsel, the court entered default judgments and held a damages hearing. Final judgment entered September 26, 2008, awarded Horowitz $166,924.04 and claimant Michelle Pagano $65,000, and ordered five years of injunctive and non-monetary relief.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main lawsuit, Title VII harassment/constructive discharge claims, default posture, and injunctive relief, but it adds unsupported details and contradicts the reference on the judgment date and monetary award amount while omitting attorney fees.

cases.case_name: Equal Employment Opportunity Comm v. David Lerner Assoc, Inc.
cases.reference: The EEOC Boston Area Office brought an action against David Lerner Associations, Inc. Together with four female employees, the EEOC alleged sex discrimination in violation of Title VII. The complaint contained allegations of a hostile work environment, including unwelcome touching, offensive comments and gestures, and threat of retaliation. After the court denied a motion to dismiss, the parties settled via a three-year consent decree. The defendants agreed to pay a total of $1.5 million in damages.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued David Lerner Associates, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the District of Connecticut on February 17, 2005, alleging Title VII sex discrimination, sexual harassment, retaliation, and constructive discharge affecting Shawna Mead, Barbara Mrozik, Susan Sheehan, Rose Carroll, and similarly situated female employees. Sheehan, Mead, Carroll, and later Mrozik intervened, asserting related claims based on alleged harassment at DLA’s Darien, Connecticut office, including unwelcome touching, sexual comments, solicitations, threats, and retaliation after complaints. On October 27, 2005, Judge Mark R. Kravitz denied DLA’s motion to dismiss or strike, holding that the EEOC had adequately attempted conciliation and could pursue “unwelcome touching” allegations. The case was resolved by a consent decree entered October 6, 2006, which included employee notice and a claims process for women employed in the Darien office from August 1, 2001 through entry of the decree; the docket lists the case as terminated that same day.

scorecard.rationale: The summary is clear and mostly consistent with the reference, but it adds several unsupported specifics and omits key settlement terms, especially the three-year consent decree and $1.5 million damages payment.

cases.case_name: Service Women’s Action Network v. U.S. Department of Defense
cases.reference: In 2015, an advocacy organization for women in military service filed this lawsuit against the United States Department of Defense (“DOD”) in the U.S. District Court for the District of Connecticut. The plaintiffs sued under Freedom of Information Act alleging that the defendant refused to provide requested documents. On June 17, 2016, the parties settled and agreed to have this case dismissed with prejudice.
cases.summary: On February 3, 2015, Service Women’s Action Network, the ACLU, and the ACLU of Connecticut sued the U.S. Department of Defense in the U.S. District Court for the District of Connecticut, No. 3:15-cv-137. The complaint alleged DOD violated FOIA by failing to conduct reasonable searches and timely produce records from West Point, the Air Force Academy, and the Naval Academy concerning admissions policies, women’s underrepresentation, recruiting, facilities, and sexual harassment and assault. During the case, DOD reported producing thousands of pages of responsive records, including about 2,300 pages from West Point, nearly 6,000 pages from the Naval Academy, and 2,711 pages from the Air Force Academy, with some redactions under FOIA Exemptions 5 and 6. The parties settled, and on June 17, 2016, they stipulated to dismissal with prejudice; the case is closed.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the FOIA suit against DOD in the District of Connecticut and its June 17, 2016 dismissal with prejudice, though it adds numerous detailed facts not present in the reference.

cases.case_name: Quarles v. General Investment
cases.reference: This putative class action lawsuit was brought by African-American employees of General Investment & Development Co.,  Windsor Property Management Co., or Windsor Investment Co. Plaintiffs sought injunctive relief, back pay, punitive and compensatory damages, and attorney's fees, alleging that Defendants had engaged in a pattern and practice of discrimination on the basis of race. The parties reached a confidential settlement agreement, and on April 15, 2003, the court approved the parties' stipulation of dismissal.
cases.summary: On June 28, 2002, Sheila Quarles, Ebony Thomas, Tammy Rogers, and Anthony Bellamy sued General Investment & Development Co., Windsor Property Management Co./Windsor Management Co., and Windsor Investment Co. in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia. They alleged a putative class action for race discrimination in promotions and job opportunities under Title VII on behalf of African-American employees, with additional individual claims including Quarles’s age discrimination claim under the ADEA and D.C. Human Rights Act claims addressed in the amended pleadings. On March 10, 2003, Judge Reggie B. Walton denied defendants’ dismissal motion as to Quarles’s Title VII claims, deferred rulings on class certification, venue for other named plaintiffs, and DCHRA issues, and ordered limited discovery. The case ended on April 15, 2003, when the court approved the parties’ confidential settlement and stipulation: the named plaintiffs’ individual claims were dismissed with prejudice, and the class claims were dismissed without prejudice.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the basic parties, race-discrimination class allegations, and settlement dismissal, but it adds many unsupported procedural and claim-specific details and omits the requested relief described in the reference.

cases.case_name: Collins v. Powell
cases.reference: A black, middle-aged, female employee sued the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation alleging race, age, and gender discrimination in promotions and on-the-job treatment. It appears the case ended in a settlement agreement, although it was not publicly available at the time of this writing.
cases.summary: Linda A. Collins, a 59-year-old Black female FDIC employee, sued Donald E. Powell, Chairman of the FDIC in his official capacity, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia on November 15, 2004. She alleged Title VII and ADEA claims for race, sex, and age discrimination, hostile work environment, retaliation, denial of promotions, discriminatory performance appraisals, denial of awards, discipline, and involuntary reassignment; the docket listed a $300,000 demand. The FDIC answered on March 7, 2005, denying discrimination and asserting defenses including failure to state a claim, legitimate nondiscriminatory reasons, and failure to exhaust administrative remedies. After discovery disputes and mediation/settlement discussions, Collins filed a notice of voluntary dismissal on March 3, 2008, and the case was terminated that day.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the basic parties and discrimination allegations and is clearly written, but it adds many unverified specifics and frames the outcome as a voluntary dismissal rather than the referenced apparent settlement.
