cases.case_name: Baldwin v. Panetta
cases.reference: Plaintiffs, two female Army Reserve officers, sued the Department of Defense, alleging that the exclusion of women from direct combat units violates their equal protection rights under the Fifth Amendment.  This is an ongoing case.
cases.summary: Command Sergeant Major Jane P. Baldwin and Colonel Ellen L. Haring sued Defense Secretary Leon Panetta, Army Secretary John McHugh, Lt. Gen. Thomas P. Bostick, and Assistant Army Secretary Thomas R. Lamont in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia on May 23, 2012. They challenged Department of Defense and Army policies excluding women from certain ground-combat units and positions, alleging violations of the Administrative Procedure Act and equal protection under the Fifth Amendment. Defendants moved to dismiss on September 13, 2012, but the case was later stayed while new regulations on women’s assignment to combat positions were implemented. Plaintiffs voluntarily dismissed the case without prejudice, and Judge Rosemary M. Collyer approved dismissal and closed the case on November 5, 2013.

scorecard.rationale: The summary clearly states the parties and core combat-exclusion equal protection challenge, but it adds unverified details and directly contradicts the reference by saying the case was voluntarily dismissed and closed rather than ongoing.

cases.case_name: Equal Employment Opportunity Commission v. Providence Hospital
cases.reference: On December 9, 2013, the EEOC filed a lawsuit in U.S. District Court for the District of Washington, D.C. on behalf of a disabled medical assistant against her employer, a hospital, alleging that the hospital violated the Americans With Disabilities Act and Title I of the Civil Rights Act by discriminating against the employee due to her disability. On August 12, 2014, the EEOC filed a stipulation of dismissal because the medical assistant, who the EEOC had filed the lawsuit on behalf of, had unexpectedly passed away on June 9, 2014, due to an automobile accident. The case is now closed.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Providence Hospital in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia on December 9, 2013, under Title I of the ADA and the Civil Rights Act of 1991. The complaint alleged Providence failed to reasonably accommodate former Medical Assistant Louise McFadden’s disability by refusing to let her work while using a cane or reassign her, then terminated her on August 24, 2011 after her FMLA leave expired. The EEOC sought injunctive relief, back pay, compensatory damages, punitive damages, reinstatement or front pay, and costs. On August 12, 2014, the parties stipulated to dismissal with prejudice after McFadden died unexpectedly in a June 9, 2014 automobile accident, with each side bearing its own costs and attorneys’ fees; the case was terminated August 13, 2014.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core parties, filing date, claims, dismissal reason, and closure, but includes numerous specific details not present in the reference, making some assertions unsupported despite fluent prose.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. I.G. BURTON OF SEAFORD
cases.reference: The EEOC filed this Age Discrimination in Employment Act case against I.G. Burton of Seaford alleging that the defendant had fired an employee due to his age. The parties entered into a three-year consent decree. Under the decree, the defendant agreed to pay $70,000 in damages to three former employees and provide training and take other steps to prevent further violations.

No. 06-524-SLR, with the docket showing the complaint filed on August 24, 2006. The EEOC alleged violations of the Age Discrimination in Employment Act, claiming I.G. Burton terminated Blair McMasters, age 75, and similarly situated runners/drivers on or shortly before their 75th birthdays because of age. cases.case_name: EEOC v. NABSTAR LLC (DBA SLEEP INN)
cases.reference: The EEOC sued a hotel chain that had replaced a 74-year-old employee who performed satisfactorily with a 28-year-old under the Age Discrimination in Employment Act. The parties entered into a two-year consent decree that required the hotel chain to pay $10,000 in damages and comply with ADEA requirements.
cases.summary: On June 8, 2005, the EEOC sued Nabstar, LLC d/b/a Sleep Inn in the U.S. District Court for the District of Delaware, Civil Action No. 05-00374-GMS. The EEOC alleged under the Age Discrimination in Employment Act that Nabstar terminated 74-year-old breakfast attendant Islyn A. Palmer in August 2003 and replaced her with a 28-year-old employee; Nabstar denied liability. The case ended on August 28, 2006, when Judge Gregory M. Sleet entered a consent decree resolving the EEOC’s claims without an admission or merits finding. Nabstar agreed to pay Palmer $10,000, refrain from age discrimination and retaliation, post notices, revise and distribute age-discrimination policies, and provide ADEA training, with the decree to remain in effect for two years.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the EEOC ADEA claim, the replacement of the 74-year-old employee by a 28-year-old, the consent decree outcome, $10,000 payment, and two-year compliance obligations in clear professional prose.

cases.case_name: Gilder v. Sallie Mae Bank
cases.reference: In 2020, an African-American individual who had his job offer from Sallie Mae Bank rescinded filed this putative class-action lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of Delaware. The plaintiff alleged that Sallie Mae Bank's policy and practice of rescinding or terminating employment based on background history is in violation of Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. This case is ongoing.
cases.summary: Willie Gilder sued Sallie Mae Bank in the U.S. District Court for the District of Delaware on July 8, 2020, seeking to bring a class action and demanding a jury trial and $5,000,000. The complaint alleges Title VII race discrimination based on Sallie Mae’s background-check policies, claiming the bank rescinded Gilder’s offer for a Collector I position after relying on an old manslaughter conviction and that the policy disparately impacts African American applicants. Gilder alleges he filed a DDOL charge on July 5, 2018, received an EEOC right-to-sue notice on April 9, 2020, and timely filed suit. As of the docket provided, Sallie Mae waived service, appeared through counsel, disclosed SLM Corporation as its corporate parent, and on September 2, 2020 received an extension of time to respond; no dismissal, settlement, or merits ruling is shown.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the venue, class-action Title VII challenge to Sallie Mae’s background-history employment policy and ongoing posture, though it adds many specific details not present in the reference.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Autozone Stores
cases.reference: In 2006, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) and the complainant, who intervened to become a plaintiff, brought this suit against Autozone, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Florida. The complaint alleged sex discrimination in violation of Title VII. The case ended in a consent decree.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued AutoZone, Inc. on September 29, 2006 in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Florida, Jacksonville Division, and former employee Eva Crawford intervened. The EEOC and Crawford alleged Title VII sex discrimination/sexual harassment and retaliation at AutoZone’s Starke, Florida store, claiming Parts Sales Manager Steve Wilber made repeated sexually offensive comments and that AutoZone terminated Crawford on January 12, 2006 after she complained; Crawford also asserted Florida Civil Rights Act claims. AutoZone denied liability, and its motion for summary judgment was denied on July 1, 2008. The case settled by consent decree entered December 24, 2008, requiring two years of injunctive relief, training, posting, and reporting, and payment of $150,000 total: $71,343.56 to Crawford, $48,656.44 to her counsel, and $30,000 to Regina Carter.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the core parties, court, Title VII sex-discrimination claim, intervention, and consent-decree outcome, but it adds many detailed allegations and procedural/monetary facts not supported by the reference.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Associated Security Enforcement, Inc.
cases.reference: Miami District Office of the EEOC brought this suit against Associated Security Enforcement, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Florida alleging discrimination on the basis of sex in violation of Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Specifically, the complaint alleged that the defendant subjected the charging parties, female employees, to a sexually hostile work environment consisting of unwelcome sexual comments and requests for sex.  In addition, the defendant retaliated against one of the charging parties by demoting her and eventually terminating her employment as a result of her rejections of his sexual advances and/or her complaints of the harassment. Three charging parties intervened in the suit in March 2005. The court enjoined the defendants from these practices and granted equitable relief. A jury trial awarded damages to the plaintiffs.
cases.summary: On Sept. 28, 2004, the EEOC sued Associated Security Enforcement, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Florida, Tallahassee Division, alleging Title VII sex-based sexual harassment and retaliation involving Kimeny Billington, Ellen Glass, and other female employees. Glass, Billington, and Karen Stewart intervened, asserting Title VII, Florida Civil Rights Act, inducement-to-prostitution, negligent-retention/supervision, and battery claims; defendant Robert Fannin was dismissed on Mar. 1, 2006, and Associated Security defaulted on liability. After a two-day damages trial, the jury awarded a total $1.34 million: emotional-distress damages of $35,000 each to two women and $25,000 each to two women, $300,000 in punitive damages for each of Billington, Glass, Stewart, and Lydia Hall, plus about $17,000 in back pay for two women. Judgment entered July 7, 2006, and on Feb. 5, 2007, the court granted permanent injunctive relief requiring anti-harassment/retaliation measures, training, notices, positive references, record expungement, and apology letters.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core EEOC Title VII sexual harassment/retaliation suit, intervention, injunction, and jury damages, but adds many unsupported specifics and omits some reference details such as the demotion/termination retaliation facts.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. B.J.'s Wholesale Club
cases.reference: In September 2008, the EEOC brought suit against BJ's Wholesale Club alleging the creation of a hostile work environment due to unlawful discrimination on the basis of national origin and race. In 2008, the parties reached a settlement that required the defendant to pay monetary damages to the plaintiff and to develop an anti-discrimination policy, post and distribute notices of equal employment opportunity rights, provide annual training on equal employment laws to its managers and supervisors, keep records of complaints of discrimination, and provide bi-annual reports to the EEOC of the progress. Additionally, the defendant agreed to pay $100,000 to the two charging parties. The case is closed.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued BJ’s Wholesale Club, Inc. in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Florida, Miami Division, on September 28, 2006, alleging Title VII race and national-origin discrimination. The complaint alleged that at BJ’s Homestead, Florida location, manager Henry Gallardo subjected Jesus Santos, who is Puerto Rican, and Black employees including William Floyd Lundy, Tarell/Ternell Miller, and Lonnie Brady to racial and national-origin slurs, creating a hostile work environment that management failed to correct. After discovery disputes, failed mediation, and a pending BJ’s summary-judgment motion, the parties settled. On March 31, 2008, the court approved a three-year consent decree requiring BJ’s to pay $100,000 total—$80,000 to Santos and $20,000 to Lundy—and to adopt anti-discrimination policies, training, posting, reporting, and recordkeeping obligations, without admitting wrongdoing.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the core parties, discrimination/hostile-environment claims, settlement payment, and injunctive obligations, but it contradicts the reference on the filing date and adds many unsupported specifics while omitting that the case is closed.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. CNC Developments, Inc.
cases.reference: On September 29, 2005, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission ("EEOC") filed a lawsuit in the Middle District Court of Florida, under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, against CNC Developments, Inc.  The EEOC sought injunctive relief, compensatory damages, punitive damages, and other forms of affirmative relief claiming that the defendant terminated the complainant's employment after its management found out she was married to an African American.  On March 30, 2007, after little substantive litigation, the District Court entered a consent decree where the defendant, among other things, agreed to pay the complainant $27,500.
cases.summary: On September 29, 2005, the EEOC sued CNC Developments, Inc., d/b/a CNC Investments, Ltd., in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Florida, Tampa Division. The EEOC alleged Title VII race discrimination, claiming CNC terminated Michelle Calos, a white employee, on or about September 29, 2004 because she was married to or associated with a Black man. CNC denied the allegations. On March 30, 2007, the court approved a consent decree closing the case; CNC agreed to two years of injunctive relief, anti-discrimination policies, training, reporting, notices, and payment of $27,500 in lost wages to Calos.

scorecard.rationale: The summary accurately captures the lawsuit, Title VII race-association termination claim, consent decree, and $27,500 payment, though it adds several specific details not present in the reference.

cases.case_name: EEOC v. Dias Landscapes
cases.reference: The EEOC sued Dias Landscapes on March 30, 2006, alleging Title VII violations. On December 15, 2006, the parties entered a three-year consent decree. The case was closed on January 5, 2007, but the Court retained jurisdiction to enforce the terms of the consent decree, set to last for three years. Since there is not further activity on the docket, this case is presumably closed.
cases.summary: The EEOC sued Dias Landscapes Corp. in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Florida, West Palm Beach Division, on March 30, 2006, alleging Title VII race and national-origin discrimination. The EEOC claimed Dias segregated its workforce and terminated Black Haitian employees, including Auguste Jean-Hilaire, Charles Moises/Moise, and Odolphe Raymond, because of their race and Haitian national origin; those three later intervened as plaintiffs. The case settled after mediation, and Judge Donald M. Middlebrooks entered a consent decree on December 15, 2006. Dias agreed to pay $150,000 total to the charging parties and two claimants, adopt anti-discrimination policies, provide training, post notices, and report compliance for three years; the case was closed on January 4, 2007, with the court retaining jurisdiction to enforce the decree.

scorecard.rationale: The summary captures the main lawsuit, Title VII claim, consent decree, and retained jurisdiction, but it adds many unsupported specifics and contradicts the reference by giving the closure date as January 4 rather than January 5, 2007.
